22.6: The specific crime of “taking pledges (haval) from your brothers for nothing” reflects the well-documented ancient Near Eastern practice of accepting a debtor’s cloak or tool as security for a loan. Mosaic Law explicitly regulated this: Exod 22:26–27 required that a man’s outer garment taken as a pledge be returned before nightfall because it was his only covering. Deut 24:12–13 expanded this protection. The Hammurabi Code (ca. 1754 BCE, Laws 66–67) regulated pledge-taking from debtors. Eliphaz accuses Job of taking pledges “for nothing” (hinnam) — without legitimate cause — the same word used in 1:9 (the adversary’s “does Job fear God for nothing?”) and 2:3 (YHWH’s admission that Job was ruined “for nothing”). The ironic echo of hinnam connects Eliphaz’s false accusation to the book’s actual theological crux.
22.7: Withholding water from the weary and bread from the hungry are the quintessential violations of ANE hospitality ethics. The duty to provide water and food to travelers and the needy was not merely a moral custom but a legal obligation in many ancient Near Eastern societies. The Egyptian negative confession in the Book of the Dead (Spell 125) includes among the declarations of innocence: “I have not been deaf to words of truth; I have not stopped my ears against words of right and wrong; I have not taken away the food-offerings in the temples; I have not held back the bread of the gods.” The categories Eliphaz uses (vv6–9) correspond almost exactly to the Egyptian negative-confession inventory of social crimes that a righteous person must deny — Eliphaz is constructing what amounts to a reverse negative-confession for Job.
22.8: The contrast between the powerful man (’ish zero’a, man of arm/power) who possesses the land and the honored man who dwells in it versus the fate of the widow/orphan reflects the standard legal distinction in ancient Near Eastern society between those with social power and those without legal standing. In ANE legal systems, widows (almanot) and orphans (yetomim) had no male advocate to represent them in legal proceedings. The king was in theory their protector (Ps 68:5: “father to the fatherless, protector of widows, is God in his holy habitation”; 1 Kgs 8:45). Eliphaz accuses Job of exploiting precisely this structural vulnerability.
22.9: The specific accusations against Job concerning widows sent away empty-handed and orphans whose arms were crushed follow the standard ANE social-justice indictment pattern. The identical categories appear in prophetic indictments: Isa 1:17 (“seek justice, correct oppression, bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow’s cause”); Isa 10:2 (the wicked who “turn aside the needy from justice and rob the poor of my people of their right, that widows may be their spoil, and that they may make the fatherless their prey”); Ezek 22:7 (“father and mother are treated with contempt; the sojourner suffers extortion; the fatherless and widow are wronged”). This catalog is the standard accusation genre; Eliphaz has applied it to Job with no evidence because his theology demands that Job must have committed such crimes.
22.10: The snares and sudden terror that surround Job are now reinterpreted by Eliphaz as logical consequences of his supposed wickedness. The word pah (snare/trap) was used in wisdom literature for the traps that the wicked set for others (Prov 22:5; Ps 91:3) and that eventually trap the wicked themselves. Eliphaz’s logic is tight within the retributive framework: the sufferings Job experiences are precisely the categories of harm that the socially oppressive person brings on others, now returned to the oppressor.
22.11: The darkness and flood imagery in v10–11 uses the standard ANE motifs of cosmic chaos overtaking the wicked: “darkness so you cannot see” and “a flood of water covers you” are the conditions of the pre-creation chaos state (Gen 1:2: darkness over the deep; waters covering everything). The return of the wicked to the chaos-state is a common theme in both Mesopotamian theological poetry (the punishment of the wicked by chaos-reversal) and in biblical poetry (Ps 18:4–5: the cords of Sheol and torrents of destruction). For Eliphaz, Job’s present darkness and flood-like suffering are the natural consequences of his social wickedness.
22.12: Eliphaz accuses Job of thinking God is too distant to observe his wickedness: “Is not God high in the heavens? See the highest stars, how lofty they are!” (v12). This implicitly charges Job with a practical form of divine-absence theology: acting as though God’s exaltation means his distance from human affairs. The Babylonian wisdom text Ludlul bel nemeqi contains a parallel concern: “I wish I knew that these things were pleasing to one’s god. What is proper to oneself is an offence to one’s god.” In both Mesopotamian theology and Eliphaz’s argument, the question of whether the divine is truly present to and concerned with human moral conduct is a central theological challenge.
22.13: “What does God know?” — Eliphaz attributes this implicit skepticism to Job, positioning Job as holding a theology of divine ignorance (v13) or inaccessibility (v14: the thick clouds are his covering, he cannot see). This philosophical position — that the gods are too exalted to be aware of or concerned with human particulars — was a recognized theological position in the ancient world. Epicurus later made the divine indifference to human affairs a formal philosophical doctrine. The OT consistently argued against this: Ps 11:4 (“the LORD is in his holy temple; the LORD’s throne is in heaven; his eyes see, his eyelids test the children of man”); Prov 15:3 (“the eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch on the evil and the good”).
22.14: The “thick clouds” (avei shehaqim) as a covering for God repeats the theophany language familiar from Sinai (Exod 19:9: “I am coming to you in a thick cloud”; Ps 18:11–12: “He made darkness his covering, his canopy around him, thick clouds dark with water”). Eliphaz turns this theophanic imagery against Job: the clouds that manifest divine presence in theophany become, in Eliphaz’s accusation, the excuse Job allegedly uses for thinking God cannot observe him. The irony is that the same clouds that hide God from sinners disclose God to the righteous; Eliphaz is accusing Job of the sinner’s misreading of divine hiddenness.
22.15: Eliphaz’s rhetorical question — “Will you keep to the old way that wicked men have trod?” — invokes the ancient precedent argument: the fate of the primordial wicked (me’olam, from of old) serves as warning for the present. The “old way” was a recognized rhetorical category in ancient wisdom: appeals to the precedent of the ancestors (“the ancient wisdom”, the rishon’im) authenticated moral and legal claims. Bildad appealed to it in 8:8 (“ask the former age”); now Eliphaz uses the same appeal but in the negative sense: the ancient wicked are the warning precedent.
22.16: The ancient wicked who were “snatched away before their time, their foundation poured out as a stream” likely refers to the flood generation (Gen 6–8) and/or the primordial wicked of antediluvian tradition. In the ANE, the flood was the paradigmatic act of divine judgment against a morally corrupt generation; Mesopotamian flood accounts (Atrahasis, Utnapishtim episode in Gilgamesh) similarly frame the flood as divine judgment against human wickedness. The image of a foundation poured out like a river (yitstaq nahar yesodham) suggests the collapse of a society as its moral foundation liquefies.
22.17: The accusation that the wicked said to God “Depart from us!” and “What can the Almighty do to us?” echoes Ps 10:4 (“In the pride of his face the wicked does not seek him; all his thoughts are, There is no God”) and Ps 14:1 (“the fool says in his heart, There is no God”). The statement is not intellectual atheism but practical rejection of divine moral authority. By implying that Job holds this attitude, Eliphaz places him in the category of the primordial wicked — the most severe accusation possible within the wisdom tradition’s moral taxonomy.
22.18: “Yet he filled their houses with good things” — Eliphaz acknowledges the theological problem his own argument creates: the wicked prospered materially before their destruction. This is precisely the problem that the Psalms of Asaph (Ps 73) and the Babylonian Theodicy wrestle with. Eliphaz’s solution is the temporal delay of judgment: God filled their houses first, then destroyed them. The “counsel of the wicked is far from me” disclaimer at the end of v18 has puzzled interpreters — in context, it seems to be Eliphaz disclaiming the wicked’s theology, but its exact syntactical relationship to the preceding material is debated.
22.19: The righteous seeing the judgment of the wicked and rejoicing, the innocent mocking the wicked, is a sentiment that appears in the Psalter’s treatment of divine judgment (Ps 52:6: “the righteous shall see and fear, and shall laugh at him”; Ps 58:10–11). In the ancient world, the public display of divine justice — the visible punishment of the wicked — served a social function of vindicating the righteous and confirming the moral order. The judicial execution of criminals was typically public in ancient Near Eastern societies for precisely this reason: moral deterrence and vindication of the community’s moral standards.
22.20: “Our enemy has been cut down, and what they left the fire has consumed” — the complete destruction formula (the person AND their possessions consumed) reflects the ANE category of herem or total destruction. In Israelite law, the devoted/cursed thing (herem) was totally destroyed (Josh 7:24–26 the destruction of Achan and all his property). In Mesopotamian conquest records, the Assyrian annals describe the total destruction of enemy cities: their populations killed, their structures burned, their land plowed and salted. The righteous here are imagined as witnesses to this total elimination of the wicked and their legacy.
22.21: Eliphaz’s penitential call — “Agree with God and be at peace; thereby good will come to you” — initiates a section that follows the structure of the ancient Near Eastern penitential prayer genre. Mesopotamian penitential prayers (the ershahunga genre, “prayers to appease the heart of a god”) follow a standard pattern: acknowledgment of divine power, confession of sin, prayer for mercy, and the expectation of restoration. Eliphaz offers Job the same template: submit (saken, agree/acquiesce), receive instruction (Torah), repent, and restoration will follow. The theology is internally coherent; the problem is that Job is innocent.
22.22: The call to “receive instruction from his mouth and place his words in your heart” uses Torah-instruction vocabulary. The word torah in its pre-Pentateuchal sense means “instruction, teaching” — the guidance given by a parent, sage, or teacher (Prov 1:8: “hear, my son, your father’s instruction (musar), and forsake not your mother’s teaching (torah)”). “Placing words in the heart” is the standard idiom for internalized wisdom (Deut 6:6: “these words that I command you today shall be on your heart”; Jer 31:33: the new covenant where God writes his law on the heart). Eliphaz calls Job to the fundamental wisdom posture of receptive listening.
22.23: “If you return to the Almighty you will be built up” — the Hebrew tibaneh (you will be built) uses the architectural/family-building metaphor for restoration and prosperity. The verb banah (to build) is used in the sense of establishing/building up a household, especially in the idiom “he will build you a house” (2 Sam 7:11: YHWH will build David a dynasty). The call to “remove injustice far from your tents” uses tent-dwelling imagery appropriate to the book’s patriarchal setting and echoes Job’s own use of tent imagery in later chapters.
22.24: “Lay gold in the dust and Ophir gold among the stones of the brook” — Eliphaz calls Job to devalue wealth as the condition of spiritual restoration. Ophir (’Ofir) was the legendary gold-producing region of the ancient world; 1 Kgs 9:28 describes Solomon’s trading ships returning from Ophir with 420 talents of gold (cf. 1 Kgs 10:11; 22:48; 2 Chr 8:18). The exact location of Ophir is debated (proposals include southern Arabia, East Africa/Punt, and the Indian subcontinent), but its name was synonymous with the finest gold throughout the ancient world. The call to treat Ophir gold as river-gravel is the most extreme ancient expression of material detachment.
22.25: The Shaddai (the Almighty) as “your gold and silver treasure” (“let the Almighty be your gold and your precious silver”) uses the substitution formula of wisdom theology: wisdom/God is more valuable than gold (cf. Prov 3:14: wisdom’s gain is better than silver; Prov 8:19: fruit of wisdom is better than gold; Ps 19:10: God’s law is more desirable than gold, even much fine gold). This substitution of God for precious metal is the climax of Eliphaz’s investment argument: repudiate material wealth, invest in divine relationship, receive greater returns.
22.26: The description of delighting in Shaddai and lifting the face to God uses the standard vocabulary of restored divine-human relationship. “Lifting the face” (nasa panim) is the idiom for the confident person who does not need to bow in shame (cf. Ezra 9:6: “I am ashamed to lift my face to you” in the shame of confessed sin; the Aaronic blessing in Num 6:26: “the LORD lift up his countenance upon you” = divine favor). Eliphaz offers Job the prospect of restored bilateral favor: Job lifts his face in confidence; God lifts his face in blessing.
22.27: The prayer-and-hearing formula — “you will make your prayer to him and he will hear you” — is the core of Eliphaz’s promised restoration. The penitential Psalms operate on this exact model: confession + petition = divine hearing + restoration (Ps 32:5–7; Ps 51:7–12). The Mesopotamian penitential ershahunga prayers follow the identical logic: the sufferer acknowledges his unknown sin, appeals to the god’s mercy, and expects the god’s appeasement and restoration of favor. Eliphaz is offering Job access to a recognized, tested template of spiritual restoration. The tragedy is that Job’s situation is not the one this template was designed for.
22.28: The fulfilling of vows when restoration comes follows the standard ancient Near Eastern vow-structure: a petition made under duress included a conditional vow (if you deliver me, I will offer X). When the prayer was answered, the vow was fulfilled publicly (Ps 22:25–26: “my vows I will perform before those who fear him... they shall eat and be satisfied; those who seek him shall praise the LORD”; Ps 116:14, 18: “I will pay my vows to the LORD”). Eliphaz’s promise that Job will pay his vows includes the implicit promise that Job’s restoration will be public enough to warrant public vow-fulfillment.
22.29: The cosmic scope of the restoration promise — “you will decree a thing and it will be established for you; light will shine on your ways” — is the most extraordinary element of Eliphaz’s offer. The promise that a human’s decree will be established (vataqam) echoes the vocabulary used for divine decrees in the ANE (the Mesopotamian simtum, divine decree that determines fate). To offer a human being quasi-divine decree-power upon restoration is the highest theological promise the wisdom tradition could make. “Light will shine on your ways” reverses Job’s self-description of walking in darkness (cf. 29:3: “by his light I walked through darkness”).
22.30: The social implications of restored divine favor — Job will be able to intercede for others and they will be delivered — reflects the ancient understanding that spiritual greatness created intercessory power. Abraham interceded for Abimelech (Gen 20:17); Moses interceded for Israel (Exod 32:11–14; Num 14:13–20); Samuel declared that to cease interceding would be sin (1 Sam 12:23). The phrase “the innocent man will be delivered” (nimlat nekiy-kappayim) uses the kappayim (palms-clean) phrase for moral innocence — the same term used in Ps 24:4 for those who may ascend the holy mountain. Eliphaz ends with an offer of restored mediatorial dignity: Job as intercessor for others. The supreme irony is that in the epilogue (42:7–8), this is precisely what God assigns Job to do.

23.1: Job’s response to Eliphaz’s third speech begins not with a refutation of the specific accusations (which Job will address in chs 29–31) but with a direct assertion of his desire for a formal legal encounter with God. The word merivah (complaint/contention) in v2 places the speech in the legal-dispute vocabulary: a riv (lawsuit/controversy) is a formal legal confrontation. Job is not complaining in the modern psychological sense; he is declaring his intent to pursue a legal claim. The formal distinction between lament and lawsuit is important for understanding the book’s genre.
23.2: Job’s description of his complaint as heavy (meri) with his hand heavy upon his groaning reflects the ANE understanding of oppression as physical weight. The metaphor of burden/heaviness for trouble appears throughout the ancient world’s lament literature: Ps 38:4 (“my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me”); Lam 1:14 (“my transgressions were bound into a yoke; by his hand they were fastened together”). The physical experience of suffering as weight connects the internal state to the vocabulary of physical oppression under a physical burden — a metaphor accessible to any agricultural society familiar with load-bearing.
23.3: Job’s cry — “Oh that I knew where I might find him!” (mi-yiteneni yada’ti ve’emtsa’ehu) — uses the standard longing formula mi-yiten (who will give / oh that) that appears repeatedly in Job (3:13; 6:8; 11:5; 13:5; 14:4,13; 19:23; 29:2; 31:31,35). The formula is characteristic of lament and longing throughout the Psalter and prophets (Num 11:29; 2 Sam 19:1; Ps 55:6; 119:5; Jer 9:1). The specific desire — to come to God’s dwelling place (mekhono, his fixed habitation) and present a legal case (mishpat) — reflects the ancient concept of the divine throne as the ultimate court of appeal.
23.4: Job would “arrange his case (mishpat) before him and fill his mouth with arguments (tokhahot)” — the legal vocabulary is precise. Mishpat is the formal legal case/judgment; tokhahot are formal legal arguments or rebuttals (the same word used in Prov 1:23,25,30 for wisdom’s rebuke). In ancient Near Eastern legal procedure, the petitioner presented a formal written or oral brief. The concept of presenting a legal brief before the divine judge appears in the Psalms of Innocence (Ps 17:1–2: “hear a just cause, O LORD; give ear to my prayer; from lips free of deceit! From your presence let my vindication come!”). Job is following this established genre.
23.5: Job expresses confidence that he would know what God would answer and understand what he would say to him. This confidence in the outcome of a direct divine encounter — that the encounter would vindicate rather than condemn him — is one of the boldest theological claims in the book. The Psalms of Innocence (Ps 7; 17; 26) express similar confidence: the righteous man is willing to stand before divine scrutiny because his conscience is clear. Ps 17:3: “you have tried my heart, you have visited me by night, you have tested me, and you will find nothing.”
23.6: Job’s hope that God would not “contend (yariv) against me with great power” or put “overwhelming force” (rak) into it, but instead pay attention to him, reflects the concern about the power-imbalance in the proposed legal encounter. In ancient Near Eastern royal court petitions, the petitioner before the king faced not only the legal question but also the power differential: the king could simply overrule without argument. Job hopes that God will engage the legal merits rather than simply overwhelming him with divine power. The concept of genuine legal engagement — not mere sovereign imposition — is essential to Job’s theological hope.
23.7: The “upright man could argue with him, and I would be delivered forever from my judge” — the word yosher (upright, straight) is the second element of Job’s character description from 1:1 (yashar). Job implicitly identifies himself as the upright man who could argue with God and be acquitted. The concept of permanent acquittal (lanetsa`h, forever) uses the same temporal adverb that appears in the permanence-formulae throughout Job and the Psalter (Ps 9:18; 74:3; 79:5). Job seeks not just temporary relief but permanent legal vindication that would establish his innocence definitively.
23.8: The four-directional search — east (I go forward), west (backward), north (left), south (right) — is a cosmological totality-expression: Job has searched the entire spatial extent of the universe for God and cannot locate him. The association of the four cardinal directions with the boundaries of the inhabited world (erets) was fundamental to ANE cosmology. Mesopotamian texts describe “the four quarters” (kibrat erbetti) of the earth as the scope of empire, divine control, or knowledge. The fourfold spatial formula here means: nowhere in the entire accessible cosmos can Job find God for the desired legal encounter.
23.9: The two-verse search (vv8–9) uses the six Hebrew words for the compass directions in a compressed poetic form: qedem (east/front), ’ahor (west/back), semo’l (north/left), yamin (south/right), with each direction thwarted. In the ancient spatial orientation where one faced east (qedem = “in front” = east; ’ahor = “behind” = west; left = north; right = south), the four directions covered the entire inhabited plane. God’s hiddenness — absent in every direction simultaneously — is the spatial expression of the deus absconditus (hidden God) theme that runs through the wisdom literature (cf. Isa 45:15: “truly you are a God who hides himself”).
23.10: “But he knows the way that I take; when he has tested me, I will come out as gold” — this verse pivots from the spatial search to the theological affirmation: even if Job cannot find God, God knows Job’s path. The gold-refining metaphor for divine testing appears in wisdom and psalmic literature: Ps 66:10 (“you tested us, O God; you refined us as silver is refined”); Prov 17:3 (“the crucible is for silver and the furnace is for gold, but the LORD tests hearts”). In the ancient world, the metalworker’s refinery was the most powerful available analogy for the process that transforms dross into pure metal — a process requiring intense heat that destroys impurities while preserving the genuine substance.
23.11: Job’s self-description as walking in God’s steps, keeping to his way, not turning aside — using the vocabulary of Deuteronomic path-metaphor (derek, way; natah, turning aside) — applies the normative language of covenant obedience to his own life. Deut 5:32–33: “you shall be careful therefore to do as the LORD your God has commanded you; you shall not turn aside to the right hand or to the left. You shall walk in all the way that the LORD your God has commanded you.” Job claims to have done precisely this. The claim is extraordinary in its context: a man stripped of everything maintains that he has followed the covenant path without deviation.
23.12: The statement that Job has not departed from the commandments of God’s lips (matsot sefatav, the commandments of his lips) and has valued (tsafanti, stored up as treasure) the words of his mouth more than his “appointed food” (huqqiy) echoes the wisdom tradition’s description of the divine word as food. The Deuteronomic teaching on manna (Deut 8:3: “man does not live by bread alone, but man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD”) uses the identical bread/word substitution. Jer 15:16: “your words were found, and I ate them, and your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart.” Job claims to have lived by this principle throughout his life.
23.13: “But he is unchangeable (vehu beahad, he is one/singular), and who can turn him back? What he desires, that he does” — the divine attribute of unchangeability (ehadut, oneness/singularity of purpose) is here expressed in its most absolute form. In Mesopotamian theology, the divine decrees (me in Sumerian, parsu in Akkadian) were similarly irrevocable: the “tablets of destiny” (tuppu shimati) contained the fates of gods and humans, and to possess them was to control reality itself. The theological problem Job identifies is the same one the Babylonian Theodicy wrestles with: if the divine will is absolutely sovereign and unchangeable, on what basis can human righteous conduct make any difference?
23.14: God “will complete what he appoints for me” (veyashlim huqqiy) — the word hoq (decree/portion/appointed share) reflects the ancient concept of a divinely predetermined allotment for each person. In Mesopotamian thought, each person’s fate was decided at birth by the gods; the Sumerian concept of nam (fate/destiny) was similarly fixed. The OT uses hoq for both natural ordinances (Jer 31:35–36: the fixed order of sun/moon/stars) and personal allocations (Job 14:5: “his days are determined, and the number of his months is with you, and you have appointed his limits that he cannot pass”). Job acknowledges that his suffering may be part of a decreed appointment he cannot overturn.
23.15: The terror that overcomes Job when he considers God’s sovereign determination (“therefore I am terrified at his presence; when I consider, I am in dread of him”) uses the vocabulary of theophanic dread. In ANE theophanies, the divine encounter invariably produced physical terror: Daniel fell on his face (Dan 10:9); Isaiah cried out in dread at the throne-room vision (Isa 6:5); Ezekiel fell prostrate (Ezek 1:28). The distinction between the proper awe of YHWH (yir’at YHWH) and the terror that overwhelms Job here (bahal, pahad) is subtle: Job is not a disobedient person fearing punishment but a righteous person terrified by the inscrutability of sovereign divine action.
23.16: “God has made my heart faint; the Almighty has terrified me” — the attribution of Job’s psychological state directly to divine causation (God made = ’el hirakh libbi, God softened/made-faint my heart) is consistent throughout Job with the book’s theological candor about divine agency. The Mesopotamian lament tradition similarly attributed psychological states of fear, confusion, and despair directly to divine abandonment or divine action: “my god has forsaken me and disappeared; my goddess has cut off her protection and gone away” (Ludlul, tablet II). The direct attribution of psychological suffering to divine agency is not blasphemy in the ancient wisdom tradition but frank theological acknowledgment.
23.17: Job’s concluding verse — he was not silenced by darkness nor by the thick darkness that covered his face — is a statement of persistent complaint despite the terror. In the ANE lament tradition, persisting in complaint despite divine hiddenness was itself an act of faith: to continue addressing the deity despite apparent abandonment maintained the relationship even under strain. Ps 88 — the darkest of the Psalms — ends with no resolution, only continued lament: “darkness is my closest friend.” Job’s refusal to be silenced by darkness is the behavioral manifestation of his spiritual tenacity.

24.1: Job’s opening challenge — “Why are not times kept by the Almighty, and why do those who know him never see his days?” — poses a precise theological problem. The “times” (ittim) refers to the divinely appointed moments for justice to be enacted; the “days” of the Almighty are his days of judgment (cf. “the day of YHWH” in the prophets). Job observes that God does not keep his own appointed schedule for justice: social wickedness continues unchecked, and God’s intervention does not come when expected. The Psalter echoes this complaint: Ps 10:1 (“why do you stand far off, O LORD? Why do you hide yourself in times of trouble?”); Ps 74:9–11 (“how long, O God?”).
24.2: The moving of boundary stones (gebulot) was the most serious form of agrarian fraud in the ancient Near East. Boundary stones (kudurru in Akkadian) were stone monuments planted to record land ownership; in Mesopotamia they were inscribed with elaborate divine curse formulae against anyone who moved, defaced, or destroyed them, and were placed under divine protection (typically the sun-god Shamash, patron of justice). Deut 27:17: “Cursed be anyone who moves his neighbor’s landmark.” Prov 22:28: “Do not move an ancient landmark that your fathers have set.” In a world without written deeds or land registries, the physical boundary stone was the only record of land ownership for agricultural families.
24.3: The theft of orphans’ donkeys and widows’ oxen targets the two classes of people in the ancient world who had no male advocate in legal proceedings. The paterfamilias represented the household in all legal and commercial matters; a widow or orphan without a male kinsman had no effective recourse against theft. The prophetic indictments of Israel repeatedly identified exploitation of the widow, orphan, and poor as the paradigmatic social sin (Isa 1:17,23; 10:2; Amos 2:6–7; Mic 2:1–2; Jer 7:6; Ezek 22:7). The ANE legal codes similarly recognized special protections for these vulnerable groups: the preamble to Hammurabi’s Code identifies protecting the widow and orphan as a primary royal obligation.
24.4: The poor being pushed off their road (yattu ’aniyyim miderech) reflects the ancient right-of-way conventions for the marginalized. In ancient Near Eastern cities, public paths, water sources, and market access were essential for the survival of the poor; exclusion from these constituted economic death. The phrase “the poor of the earth hide themselves together” pictures an underclass forced into collective concealment from their oppressors — a social reality documented in ancient administrative texts where the landless poor gathered in marginal areas outside settled zones.
24.5: The comparison of the dispossessed poor to pere’ (wild donkeys) “in the desert” (ba’aravah) echoes the birth oracle of Ishmael in Gen 16:12: “he will be a wild donkey of a man; his hand against everyone and everyone’s hand against him, and he will live to the east of all his kinsmen.” Both texts use the wild donkey as the paradigm of marginal existence outside the social structures of settled life. In the ancient world, the wild donkey (onager) ranged the desert steppe beyond the zone of agricultural settlement; to be compared to one was to be identified with those who existed outside the social order that guaranteed identity, security, and rights.
24.6: The poor gathering fodder (vequtsr) in a field that belongs to another is a picture of the ancient practice of gleaning — collecting the leftover grain after the formal harvest. Mosaic Law mandated that landowners leave the edges of fields and fallen grain for the poor and sojourner (Lev 19:9–10; 23:22; Deut 24:19–21). The book of Ruth portrays this gleaning practice as a survival mechanism for widows. Job’s description shows the poor reduced to gleaning in the vineyard of the wicked — subsisting on the margins of the economy of those who exploit them.
24.7: The naked poor lying without clothing through the night is one of the OT’s most direct descriptions of destitution. In the Mediterranean climate, nights in the hill country could be cold; the outer garment taken as a pledge (cf. Exod 22:26–27; Deut 24:12–13) was the poor person’s only protection against the cold. The legislation requiring the return of a pledge before nightfall was precisely because the garment served as a blanket. Job’s description of people who have no covering in the cold and no clothing to cover themselves depicts the lived consequence of the pledge-taking abuse Eliphaz falsely accused Job of committing (22:6).
24.8: Being “wet with mountain rains and cling to the rock for want of shelter” is a vivid description of exposure to the Palestinian climate. The annual rain season (October–April) included downpours; without shelter, exposure to rain in the hill country produced hypothermia. The rock as last resort shelter echoes the wilderness imagery throughout the Psalter (Ps 18:2: YHWH as rock, fortress, refuge). The irony in Job’s description is that the things YHWH provides (rock as metaphorical shelter) are the literal last resort of those whom society has failed to shelter.
24.9: The snatching of orphans from the breast reflects the practice of child-bondage for debt. When a debtor defaulted, creditors could seize children as debt-slaves in the ancient world. 2 Kgs 4:1–7 provides a direct OT example: a widow whose husband had died in debt was about to have her two sons taken by the creditor. In Mesopotamian law, debt-slavery was regulated (Hammurabi Code, Laws 117–119): a debtor who sold himself or family members into slavery was limited to a three-year term. The OT regulated but did not eliminate the practice (Exod 21:7–11; Deut 15:12–17). Job describes the unregulated, extortionate version of this practice.
24.10: The poor “go about naked, without clothing; hungry, they carry the sheaves” — the phrase captures the ultimate irony of agrarian exploitation: the people who perform the harvest labor that produces food are themselves the most food-insecure. In ancient Egypt, the peasant farmers who produced the grain stored in pharaoh’s granaries could face starvation when those granaries were controlled by the state. The Biblical Joseph story (Gen 47:13–26) describes how this system worked: all Egyptian grain eventually belonged to pharaoh. Job’s description shows the same dynamic in a private-exploitation context.
24.11: The laborers who produce olive oil between the millstones but go thirsty, who tread the winepress but suffer thirst, depicts the grotesque inversion at the heart of agricultural exploitation. In the ancient Near East, olive oil and wine were the premium agricultural products and the primary sources of surplus wealth; they were also the staples of diet (with bread). The workers who produce these products while denied their consumption suffered the deepest form of material injustice: proximity to abundance with enforced deprivation. The Mosaic Law’s prohibition on muzzling an ox while it treads grain (Deut 25:4) encoded a principle of minimal worker access to the produce of their labor.
24.12: The urban context — “from out of the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help” — shifts from agricultural to urban suffering. The “cry” (tisha’vena) of the wounded is the tzeaqah (cry of distress/injustice) — the category of cry that activates divine response in the OT: Gen 4:10 (Abel’s blood cries from the ground); Exod 3:7 (God hears the Israelites’ tzeaqah in Egypt); Exod 22:23 (widows and orphans cry and God will hear). The piercing theological irony of v12 is the final phrase: “yet God charges no one with wrongdoing (vel’o–yasim ’elohim tifla`h)” — God remains silent despite hearing the cry that should activate his judgment.
24.13: The section on “those who rebel against the light” (vv13–17) introduces the ancient moral coding of darkness and light. Throughout the ancient world, darkness was the time of social dissolution, moral transgression, and the activity of forces hostile to civilization. Mesopotamian texts associate night with the activities of demons and the underworld. In the OT, nighttime crimes are morally coded as especially sinister because they exploit the darkness to avoid divine observation (cf. Job 22:13–14 where Eliphaz attributed to Job the idea that God cannot see). The three nighttime criminals of vv14–17 — the murderer, adulterer, thief — all share the characteristic of exploiting darkness.
24.14: The murderer rising at dawn to kill the poor and needy (“with the light the murderer rises; he kills the poor and needy, and in the night he is like a thief”) inverts the associations of morning and safety. Dawn (shahar) throughout the Psalter represents divine deliverance (Ps 46:5: “God will help her when morning dawns”; Ps 30:5: “joy comes with the morning”). For the poor, dawn is instead the signal for the murderer who kills them in the transitional moment when the night-thief becomes the day-murderer. The category of “poor and needy” (’ani ve’evyon) as murder victims reflects the ancient reality that the poor had no armed protection.
24.15: The adulterer who “watches for the twilight, saying, No eye will see me, and he veils his face” reflects the ancient understanding of adultery as a crime of concealment and darkness. In the ANE legal codes, adultery was severely punished (death in Hammurabi Code, Laws 129–132; Lev 20:10; Deut 22:22). The crime required secrecy precisely because of the severe penalties; the veil that hides the adulterer’s face is the instrument of the denial that all adultery depends upon. The phrase “no eye will see me” echoes Eliphaz’s accusation that Job thought the divine eye could not see through the clouds (22:13–14), completing the ironic inversion: the wicked ACTUALLY hold this view, while Job does not.
24.16: Thieves who “break into houses in the dark; in the daytime they seal themselves up” reflects the ancient categorization of burglary as a nighttime crime. In Hammurabi’s Code (Law 21), breaking into a house was punishable by death, with execution at the site of entry. The house-breaking (hoter bayit) image appears in Exod 22:2 in the distinction between nighttime and daytime burglary (the homeowner may kill a nighttime burglar without guilt; not so in daylight). The criminals of Job 24:14–16 illustrate precisely the class of people who exploit darkness for their crimes — the same light-haters of v13.
24.17: The light-haters’ fellowship with the terrors of thick darkness (ki yaqd hoshekh lam yoter kitsalmavet, deep darkness is morning to them) inverts the normal diurnal orientation. In ANE cosmology, the transition between night and day was a moment of divine ordering (the sun-god Shamash’s daily journey was a renewal of cosmic order); the morning was the time of divine triumph over the forces of night/chaos. For the criminals of vv13–17, this diurnal order is exactly reversed: they fear the light and “know” darkness as their natural element. This reversal of the light/dark moral coding is one of the ways the ancient world identified the wicked as fundamentally disordered.
24.18: The textual and interpretive difficulties of vv18–25 have generated extensive scholarly discussion. Many commentators consider these verses as voiced by one of the friends (perhaps quoting standard wisdom doctrine) or as a misplaced text. In the flow of Job’s speech, the description of the swift destruction of the wicked (v18: “swift is he on the face of the waters”) stands in tension with the preceding description of the wicked’s apparent impunity (vv1–17). The verse may represent Job quoting or partially ironically adopting the friends’ position before refuting it, a literary device attested in ancient wisdom dialogue.
24.19: The agricultural metaphor — drought and heat consume the snow waters, so Sheol consumes those who have sinned — uses the natural process of seasonal water-loss as an analogy for the irreversible movement toward death. In the ancient world, the evaporation and absorption of water (whether by sun, heat, or dry ground) was understood as total loss — water could not be recovered once evaporated. The comparison of Sheol’s consumption of sinners to this irreversible natural process implies the finality and completeness of divine judgment: once consumed, nothing remains.
24.20: The picture of the wicked forgotten by their mother’s womb, consumed by worms, no longer remembered, broken like a tree, reflects the ancient understanding of social memory as a form of ongoing existence. In Mesopotamian theology, the dead were sustained by the libations and food-offerings of descendants; to die without descendants, or to be forgotten, was a second death beyond physical death. The inscription of names in royal records and temple archives was an attempt to defeat this second death. Job’s description of the wicked being utterly forgotten — by family, by community, by earth itself — is the ultimate negation of legacy.
24.21: The identification of the wicked as those who exploit the barren woman (who cannot produce heirs) and do no good for the widow connects the fate of the wicked back to the social crimes of vv2–9. The “barren woman” (’aqarah) in the ancient world had no social standing through children; the widow without sons had no legal advocate. Both were paradigmatic victims of exploitation in the ancient Near Eastern legal and social world. The wicked of ch24 are those who target precisely these structurally vulnerable members of society, and their own social extinction — being forgotten, without descendants — is presented as a fitting mirror-judgment.
24.22: The mighty who are dragged away (“God drags away the mighty by his power”) despite their accumulated strength (abilim) uses the vocabulary of the divine warrior who overcomes human power. In ANE royal inscriptions, the great kings boasted of their invincible strength; the OT consistently argues that divine power overturns the mightiest human powers (cf. 1 Sam 2:4: “the bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble bind on strength”; Ps 46:9: “he breaks the bow and shatters the spear”). Job appears to describe the eventual divine overthrow of the powerful wicked, but in a way that seems to contradict the sustained divine inaction of vv1–17.
24.23: The paradox of vv23–24 is striking: God gives the wicked security and rest (betah), his eyes are on their ways, they are exalted for a little while, then destroyed. This precisely encapsulates the problem of the prosperity of the wicked that the Psalter wrestles with (Ps 73:3–12: Asaph nearly loses his faith seeing the ease of the wicked; their feet stand on slippery places and they are destroyed in a moment, v18–19). The “little while” (me’at) of the wicked’s exaltation followed by their humiliation is the temporal solution to the retributive-justice problem: justice is delayed but certain.
24.24: The wicked being “brought low and gathered like all others; cut off like the heads of grain” uses the harvest-grain metaphor for death that appears throughout biblical and ANE literature. The Mesopotamian image of the god cutting down humans like grain (the Descent of Ishtar: those who die descend to the underworld where the grain is their food) and the OT image of death as reaping (Isa 17:5; Jer 9:22; Amos 7:17) both treat the agricultural metaphor of cutting as the universal fate of humanity. The wicked’s fate in v24 — cut down like grain — is distinguished only by timing, not ultimate destiny.
24.25: Job’s concluding challenge — “If it is not so, who will prove me a liar and make my speech amount to nothing?” — is a formal legal challenge to the friends’ refutation. The phrase ’im-lo mi yazimeni veyasem le’efes millatiy is a juridical formula calling for a counter-argument. In ancient Hebrew legal discourse, a statement followed by “who can prove me wrong?” was a claim to have stated a demonstrably verifiable position. Job is not expressing private theological opinion but stating what he presents as observable social fact: the wicked prosper, the innocent suffer, and God does not intervene on schedule. This is the empirical challenge that demands a response.

25.1: Bildad’s third speech is the shortest speech in the entire dialogue (6 verses) and stands in stark contrast to his first two speeches (ch8: 22 verses; ch18: 21 verses). The brevity has generated several interpretive theories: (1) the friends are running out of arguments; (2) the text is incomplete or displaced; (3) Bildad deliberately speaks briefly because Job’s overwhelming self-defense (chs 23–24) has silenced him. Whatever the cause, the speech’s brevity is structurally significant: after Job’s sustained challenge to divine justice in ch24, the friends can only retreat to abstract theology about divine transcendence.
25.2: “Dominion and fear are with God; he makes peace in his high heaven” — the divine attribute of mishal (rule/dominion) combined with pahad (fear/dread) as the foundation of cosmic order reflects the ANE theology of divine kingship. In Ugaritic texts, El’s epithet included his awesome sovereignty over the divine assembly; Mesopotamian theology described the “awesome splendor” (melammu) that radiated from the king and the gods as a physical manifestation of their power. In the OT, YHWH’s pahad (dread) is the quality that causes nations to tremble at his presence (Exod 15:16; Deut 2:25; 11:25). The celestial peace (shalom) of God’s high places contrasts with the terrestrial chaos Job has described in ch24.
25.3: The rhetorical question “Is there any number to his armies?” invokes the concept of YHWH tseva’ot (the LORD of Hosts/Armies) — one of the most common divine epithets in the OT (ca. 280 occurrences). The divine army includes the celestial bodies (Joshua 5:14: the commander of the army of YHWH; Isa 34:4: the host of heaven), the angels (Ps 103:21: “bless the LORD, all his hosts”), and the forces of creation (Ps 148:2–5: angels, sun, moon, stars, waters). The Ugaritic divine warrior Baal also commanded cosmic forces; the Assyrian king claimed that the divine armies fought alongside his human armies. Bildad’s point is the infinite scope of divine military power: any human resistance is cosmically absurd.
25.4: Bildad’s central argument: “How then can man be righteous before God? How can he who is born of woman be pure?” — the qal vahomer (light-to-heavy) argument structure: if even cosmic elements are impure before God (vv5–6), how much less any human? The phrase “born of woman” (yelud ’ishah) emphasizes the creatureliness and biological origin of humans, in contrast to the eternal, self-existent divine. Job himself uses this identical phrase in 14:1 (“man who is born of a woman is few of days and full of trouble”), but in the context of a plea for divine compassion rather than a rhetorical argument for human insignificance. The same phrase appears in Matt 11:11 / Luke 7:28 in Jesus’s description of John the Baptist.
25.5: The cosmic purity hierarchy — the moon is not bright, the stars are not pure in God’s eyes — uses the luminaries as the highest attainable standard of purity and brightness in the visible world. In the ANE, the moon and stars were divine beings or divine symbols of the highest order: Sin (moon-god) and Shamash (sun-god) in Mesopotamia; the “sons of God” (bene ha-’elohim) in the OT may include the celestial bodies. The Psalter uses the stars as symbols of divine magnificence: Ps 8:3 (“when I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place”). If even these highest visible manifestations of purity fall short of divine holiness, the qal vahomer argument is unanswerable on its own terms.
25.6: The descent from moon/stars (v5) to mortal-as-worm (v6) covers the full range of creaturely existence: from the highest visible natural objects to the lowest crawling organisms. The double worm-vocabulary — rimmah (maggot/worm, associated with death and decay in 7:5; 17:14; 21:26; 24:20) and tola’at (earthworm/crimson-worm) — emphasizes the abject organic lowliness of humanity. Ps 22:6: “But I am a worm (tola’at) and not a man, scorned by mankind and despised by the people” uses the identical first word. The “son of man” (ben ’adam) as maggot/worm is the book’s most extreme statement of human creaturely insignificance — ironically placing in Bildad’s mouth a phrase that the Psalter will apply to the suffering righteous and ultimately to the Messiah.

26.1: Job’s response begins with blistering sarcasm that inverts the conventional expressions of gratitude for counsel received. Where conventional wisdom discourse would begin with acknowledgment of the interlocutor’s helpful instruction (as Eliphaz began 22:2–4 with rhetorical questions about human-divine benefit), Job’s “how you have helped him who has no power!” turns each expected affirmation into a reproach. In the ancient wisdom tradition, formal courtesy in debate was expected; Job’s direct sarcasm represents a sustained refusal to maintain the conventional courtesies of the wisdom dialogue genre.
26.2: “How you have helped him who has no power! How you have saved the arm that has no strength!” — the ironic structure of vv2–4 is built on the same social-assistance vocabulary that Job’s own noble past (ch29) will use. In ch29:12–16, Job describes delivering the poor, saving the fatherless, strengthening the blind and lame — genuine acts of social rescue. Bildad’s speech offered Job none of this actual assistance; it offered only theoretical theology. Job’s sarcasm calls out the gap between the friends’ confident theological discourse and the total absence of practical or empathetic engagement with his actual suffering.
26.3: “How you have counseled him who has no wisdom!” — the wisdom counsel that Bildad offered is dismissed as non-counsel: what wisdom does it give to tell a suffering man that humans are maggots? In the ancient wisdom tradition, genuine counsel (’etsah) was measured by its practical usefulness to the person in need. The wisdom literature repeatedly distinguishes between counsel that actually helps (Prov 20:18: “plans are established by counsel”) and empty speech. Job’s question “and how have you declared sound wisdom abundantly?” (toushiyah, practical wisdom/competence) sarcastically frames Bildad’s abstract theology as a parody of the practical wisdom the situation required.
26.4: “With whose help have you uttered words, and whose breath has come out from you?” — this verse is a direct challenge to Bildad’s (and perhaps Eliphaz’s) claim to speak with divine authority or inspiration. Eliphaz explicitly claimed divine revelation in ch4:12–16 (a spirit passing, a whispering voice, a revelation in the night). Bildad has made no such explicit claim, but the friends speak with the authority of the received wisdom tradition. Job challenges the very source of their authority: if their words do not come from genuine encounter with divine reality, who is the breath behind their speech? The question anticipates the divine speeches (chs 38–41) where YHWH will speak from the whirlwind — genuine divine breath.
26.5: Job’s counter-hymn begins not with the heavens but with the underworld: “the Rephaim tremble below the waters, those who dwell in them” (harefa’im yeholalu mitahat mayim veshokheneyhem). The Rephaim (refa’im) appear in the OT as both: (1) the shades/ghosts of the dead in Sheol (Ps 88:10; Prov 2:18; 9:18; Isa 14:9; 26:14,19); and (2) the giant pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan (Gen 14:5; 15:20; Deut 2:10–11; 3:11). In Ugaritic texts, the Rpum (cognate of Rephaim) were the noble dead warriors who could be summoned for divine feasts. Job’s hymn begins where Bildad ended: if even the cosmic purity of the stars is impure before God (25:5), how much more are the dead in their underworld domain subject to divine sovereignty.
26.6: “Sheol is naked before God, and Abaddon has no covering” — ’Avaddon appears here as a synonym for Sheol (also 28:22; 31:12; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11; 27:20). The etymology (aved, destruction/perishing) suggests a realm of destruction; in Rev 9:11, Abaddon becomes the angel-king of the bottomless pit (“the angel of the abyss, whose name in Hebrew is Abaddon”). The claim that Sheol and Abaddon are arummim (naked/bare, with no covering) before YHWH contrasts sharply with the Mesopotamian underworld theology, where the realm of Irkalla was a semi-autonomous domain under the rule of Nergal and Ereshkigal — not subordinate to the sovereignty of the sky-god. For Job (and for OT theology generally), there is no domain beyond YHWH’s sight.
26.7: “He stretches out the north (tsafon) over the void, and hangs the earth over nothing” (toleh eretz al-belima) — this is one of the most theologically striking cosmological statements in the OT. The tsafon (north/Zaphon) was also the name of Baal’s sacred mountain in Ugaritic theology (Mount Zaphon = Jebel al-Aqra in northern Syria), the divine mountain of the assembly. Job’s use of tsafon may carry both senses: the literal north and the mythological divine mountain. The phrase toleh eretz al-belima (hanging the earth on nothingness/without support) stands in tension with other OT cosmological images of the earth resting on pillars (Ps 75:3; 1 Sam 2:8) or upon the deep (Ps 24:2). Mesopotamian cosmology described the earth as resting on the Apsu (primordial fresh water beneath the earth). Job’s formulation is unique in the ancient world in its claim that the earth has no physical support.
26.8: “He binds up the waters in his thick clouds, and the cloud is not split open under them” — the image of clouds as divine water-containers reflects the ancient understanding of clouds as the mechanism of rain delivery. In Ugaritic mythology, Baal as the storm-god was the “rider of the clouds” (rkb ’rpt); his control of rain and clouds was the basis of his agricultural importance. YHWH appropriates this imagery: Ps 104:3 (“he makes the clouds his chariot”); Nah 1:3 (“his way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet”). The marvel is that clouds hold quantities of water without rupturing — an observation about the physical world that points to divine engineering.
26.9: “He covers the face of the full moon and spreads over it his cloud” — the exact meaning of kiseh (full moon / throne / covering) in this verse is debated. If “full moon,” the verse describes God’s control over lunar visibility through cloud cover. If “throne,” the verse describes God spreading cloud as a veil over his heavenly throne. In either reading, the divine sovereignty over the upper cosmic realm is the point. The moon (Sin) in Mesopotamian theology was a major deity; for Job, the moon is simply an object under YHWH’s sovereign management.
26.10: “He has inscribed a circle on the face of the waters at the boundary between light and darkness” — the image of YHWH drawing a circular boundary (hog, inscribed limit) on the surface of the cosmic ocean reflects the ancient cosmological picture of the habitable earth as a disc encircled by the primordial ocean. Prov 8:27 uses the identical imagery: “when he established the heavens, I was there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.” The boundary between light and darkness as a divinely inscribed limit reflects the first creative act of Gen 1:3–4 (God separating light from darkness); here the separation is expressed in terms of the horizontal boundary inscribed on the cosmic waters rather than the temporal separation of day from night.
26.11: “The pillars of heaven tremble and are astounded at his rebuke” — the pillars of heaven (amudei shamayim) are the cosmological columns supporting the vault of heaven in the ancient ANE worldview. Ps 75:3: “when the earth totters and all its inhabitants, it is I who keep steady its pillars.” 1 Sam 2:8: “the pillars of the earth are YHWH’s.” In Phoenician/Canaanite cosmology, the divine mountains at the edges of the world served as heaven’s supports; in Egyptian cosmology, four pillars at the corners of the sky held up the vault. Job’s reference to these pillars trembling at divine rebuke demonstrates YHWH’s power over the very structural supports of the cosmos.
26.12: “By his power he stilled the sea; by his understanding he struck down Rahab” — Rahab (Rahav) is the primordial sea-chaos monster, parallel to Leviathan, representing the waters of disorder that God defeated in the creation conflict. Rahab appears in Ps 89:10 (“you crushed Rahab like a carcass; you scattered your enemies with your mighty arm”); Isa 51:9 (“was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces, who pierced the dragon?”); Ps 87:4 (Rahab = Egypt). In Ugaritic mythology, Baal’s victory over Yam (the sea-god) established divine order over chaos; the OT adapts this combat mythology to YHWH’s creation-act. Job’s hymn places the primordial sea-combat in the larger context of YHWH’s cosmic sovereignty over all domains.
26.13: “By his breath the skies were cleared; his hand pierced the fleeing serpent” (bariah nahash) — the “fleeing serpent” formula appears nearly verbatim in Isa 27:1: “in that day the LORD with his hard and great and strong sword will punish Leviathan the fleeing (bariah) serpent, Leviathan the twisting serpent, and he will slay the dragon that is in the sea.” The nahash bariah is the cosmic chaos-serpent of ANE mythology, equivalent to the Ugaritic Lotan (the “twisting serpent” btn ’qltn) whom Baal defeated in the Ugaritic Baal cycle. Job uses this standard mythology to assert YHWH’s victory over the chaos-powers — not Baal’s victory, but YHWH’s. The breath (nishmo) of YHWH that clears the skies also points back to Gen 2:7 (the divine breath that animated humanity).
26.14: “These are the outer fringes of his ways (qetsot derakhav); how small a whisper do we hear of him! Who could understand the thunder of his power?” — this concluding verse is one of the most profound epistemological statements in the book. All the cosmic wonders just catalogued — the conquered Rephaim, the naked Sheol, the suspended earth, the cloud-bound water, the circular horizon, the trembling pillars, the defeated Rahab, the pierced serpent — these are only the outer edge, the margins, the fringe (qetsot) of YHWH’s actual ways. The Ludlul bel nemeqi similarly concludes with a statement of divine incomprehensibility: “who can know the will of the gods in heaven? Where has confounded mortal ever learned what a god’s plan is?” Job’s hymn surpasses Bildad’s ch25 speech in comprehensiveness, grandeur, and humility — demonstrating that Job understands divine majesty better than his accusers.

15.1: Eliphaz opens the second dialogue cycle (chs 15–21). The first cycle (chs 4–14) saw the Friends argue from general retribution principles and Job respond with increasingly direct challenges to God. The second cycle shifts: the Friends become more personal and accusatory, and their speeches become less philosophical and more polemical. Eliphaz, who opened the first cycle with gentleness (4:2: “can we hold back our words?”), now opens with pointed attack. The cycle structure — three rounds of speeches with Job responding to each — is one of the most architecturally sophisticated features of the book.
15.2: “Should a wise man answer with empty words, filling himself with the hot east wind?” Eliphaz attacks Job’s wisdom credentials immediately. Rûaḥ qāḏîm (hot east wind) is the desert sirocco — scorching, desiccating, destructive. In the ancient Near East, the east wind was associated with destruction (Gen 41:6,23 — the east wind withered the good grain in Pharaoh’s dream; Ezek 17:10; Hos 13:15). To be “full of the east wind” is to be full of hot, damaging, empty talk. Eliphaz is reversing Job’s wisdom-credentials: Job claimed to have the wisdom the Friends lack (12:3; 13:2); Eliphaz now claims Job speaks hot wind, not wisdom.
15.3: “Should he argue with worthless words, with speeches that accomplish nothing?” The two criteria Eliphaz applies are ḏāḇār lōʾ-yiskōn (words that profit nothing) and millîm lōʾ-yôʿîl (speeches that do not benefit). These are wisdom-pragmatic criteria: useful speech versus useless speech. In the wisdom tradition, words that do not benefit the hearer and speaker are categorically worthless (Prov 10:19; 15:2; 18:21). Eliphaz is applying the wisdom tradition’s own criteria against Job’s complaint-speech. The irony: wisdom speech is being used to silence wisdom-speech.
15.4: “You are actually undermining the fear of God and hindering prayer before him.” This is Eliphaz’s most serious theological charge: Job’s speeches threaten the foundational structures of religion. Yirʾat ʾĕlôah (fear of God) is the book’s programmatic term — Job is described as fearing God in 1:1,8,9; 2:3. To undermine the fear of God is to attack the foundation of wisdom (28:28) and covenant-fidelity. Śîaḥ (prayer/meditation) before God — Eliphaz accuses Job of making prayer itself seem pointless. This charge accurately describes the social danger of Job’s position even if it misrepresents Job’s intent.
15.5: “Your mouth reveals your own guilt; you have adopted the tongue of the cunning.” ʿᵃwōneḵā (your guilt/iniquity) is revealed by the mouth itself — the speech-as-evidence principle. Lᵉšôn ʿᵃrûmîm (the tongue of the cunning/clever) — the word ʿārûm is the same used for the serpent in Gen 3:1. By accusing Job of serpentine cleverness, Eliphaz is casting him as a tempter who seduces others away from God. This is the escalation from ch4–5: there Eliphaz merely suggested Job had some hidden fault; now he accuses Job of actively subverting faith.
15.6: “Your own mouth condemns you, not I; your very lips bear witness against you.” Eliphaz steps back to appear impartial: he is not the prosecutor but the observer of Job’s self-incrimination. This is the ancient legal principle that spontaneous speech could be used as evidence (cf. Prov 12:13: “by the transgression of his lips an evil man is ensnared”). In Eliphaz’s framework, Job’s complaints — especially his direct challenges to God — are the evidence of the guilt that originally produced the suffering. The circular logic here is what Job will spend the rest of the dialogue exposing.
15.7: “Are you the first man who was ever born? Were you shaped before the hills?” Eliphaz invokes the rîʾšôn ʾāḏām (first/primordial man) tradition — the mythological figure of a pre-existent human who witnessed creation. This figure appears in Ezekiel’s oracle against the prince of Tyre (Ezek 28:12–14: “you were the model of perfection, full of wisdom... you were in Eden, the garden of God”) and has parallels in Ugaritic texts describing El and Baal as the eldest of the primordial figures. The hills (gᵉḇāʿôṯ) predate human civilization in biblical cosmology (Prov 8:25). To be born before the hills would mean predating creation itself — wisdom’s own territory (Prov 8:22–25).
15.8: “Have you been present in God’s secret council? Do you think wisdom belongs to you alone?” The sôḏ (secret council, intimate assembly) of God is a significant concept in OT prophecy. Jeremiah 23:18,22 uses it as the criterion distinguishing true from false prophets: “who has stood in the council of the LORD?” Amos 3:7 notes that God reveals his plans to his servants the prophets. The divine council appears in Job’s prologue (1:6–12; 2:1–6) and in 1 Kings 22:19–23 (Micaiah’s vision). Eliphaz is mockingly suggesting Job claims council-access — wisdom as his personal possession. The irony: Job’s suffering was in fact decided in the divine council, though Job does not know this.
15.9: “What do you know that we do not? What insight have you that we lack?” The epistemological challenge — mah-yadaʿtā (what do you know?) — mirrors Job’s own challenge in 12:3 (“I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you”). Eliphaz turns Job’s epistemological confidence back against him. In the wisdom tradition, knowledge claims are to be tested against the tradition of the elders (v10), personal experience, and the instruction of the community. Eliphaz is marshaling all three against Job.
15.10: “Among us are both the gray-haired and the very aged, men far older than your father.” The Friends’ authority claim: tradition mediated through age. In ancient Near Eastern wisdom, śêḇāh (gray hair) and advanced age were marks of authentic wisdom-transmission. The Instruction of Amenemope, the Instructions of Ptahhotep, and Mesopotamian wisdom texts all appeal to the sage’s great age as credential. Job’s father is presumably Eliphaz’s contemporary; by claiming superiority over Job’s father’s generation, Eliphaz claims the highest tier of traditional authority. Job will later be vindicated over these very elders (42:7–9).
15.11: “Are God’s consolations not enough for you — the gentle word he has offered you?” Tanḥûmôṯ ʾēl (consolations of God) — Eliphaz refers to his own counsel as divine consolation. The claim is extraordinary: the Friends are offering God’s own comfort through their speeches. Wᵉḏāḇār laʾaṭ ʿimmāḵ (the gentle word with you) — the Friends’ initial approach was indeed gentle (2:13: seven days of silence). But Job’s rejection of their comfort has escalated the confrontation. Eliphaz is partly right that the comfort was genuine in intent; what he misses is that it was theologically inadequate to Job’s actual situation.
15.12: “Why does your heart carry you away? Why do your eyes flare?” Mah-yiqqāḥᵃḵā libbᵉḵā (what carries away/seduces your heart?) — the heart as the seat of volition and moral judgment in ANE psychology. When the heart is “carried away,” it has lost its grounding in received wisdom and sound judgment. The eye-flash (wᵉmah-yirzemûn ʿênêḵā) may refer to the glittering look of anger or spiritual arrogance — the outward sign of an inner state that has become unmoored from the tradition. Eliphaz diagnoses Job’s problem as psychological/spiritual disorientation rather than theological error.
15.13: “That you turn your spirit against God and pour out such words from your mouth?” Tāšêḇ ʾel-ʾēl rûḥeḵā (you turn your spirit against God) — the direct accusation of spiritual antagonism toward God. In the wisdom tradition, the spirit (rûaḥ) aligned with God is the source of life and wisdom; a spirit turned against God is dangerous and self-destructive. Wᵉḥôṣēʾṯā mippîḵā millîn (and you bring out such words from your mouth) — the progression is clear: the turned spirit produces the dangerous words. Eliphaz’s diagnosis frames Job’s theological challenge as spiritual rebellion rather than honest protest.
15.14: “What is a mortal man, that he could be clean? Can one born of woman be righteous?” This verse reprises Eliphaz’s first-cycle assertion (4:17: “Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can even a strong man be more pure than his Maker?”) but with different framing: not righteousness relative to God but the inherent impurity of being yᵉlûḏ ʾiššāh (born of woman). The phrase “born of woman” is a standard idiom for mortal human frailty (Job 14:1; Ps 51:5; Gal 4:4). Eliphaz is arguing from creatureliness: the category “creature born of woman” precludes the category “righteous before God.”
15.15: “God does not even trust his holy ones; the heavens themselves are not clean in his sight.” The “holy ones” (qᵉḏōšāyw) are the members of the divine council — the heavenly beings who surround God’s throne (Job 1:6: “the sons of God”; Ps 82:1; Dan 4:13). If even these are not trusted by God, how much less a human? This argument escalates the scale: from the Friend’s first-cycle point (4:18: even angels are found wanting) to now making the heavens themselves impure. The cosmological scale of divine holiness makes human righteousness-claims look absurd. Qumran community texts later developed this heavenly-purity theology extensively.
15.16: “How much more abominable and vile is man, who laps up wickedness like water!” The qal wāḥōmer (light-and-heavy) argument from v15: if heavenly beings are not trusted, ʾap kî (how much more) the creature who drinks iniquity like water. Niteʿāḇ wᵉneʾᵃlāḥ (abominable and vile) — the strongest words for moral corruption, used elsewhere in contexts of idolatry and sexual sin (Deut 24:4; Ps 14:1). Eliphaz’s rhetoric has intensified enormously from his gentle opening in 4:2–6. The second cycle reveals how the Friends’ theodicy, under pressure, moves toward denunciation.
15.17: “I will show you — listen to me.” The instruction formula (šimʿāh-lî, listen to me) transitions Eliphaz from accusation to teaching. What follows (vv18–35) is the fate-of-the-wicked poem that occupies the bulk of ch15. By prefacing it with personal testimony (“what I have seen”) and traditional authority (“what wise men have declared,” v18), Eliphaz frames his description of the wicked as both empirically and traditionally validated. The implicit application to Job makes this the most extensive indirect accusation in the book.
15.18: “What wise men have declared, passed down from their fathers without concealment.” The appeal to unbroken tradition (miʾᵃḇôṯām lōʾ-kiḥēḏû, from their fathers without concealment) is the classic wisdom-authority claim: accumulated experiential knowledge, faithfully transmitted across generations. This contrasts with Job’s implied claim to direct insight that overrides tradition. The wisdom literature of Egypt and Mesopotamia similarly invokes the chain of transmission: Ptahhotep opens with the wisdom of ancestors; Amenemope addresses his son as recipient of the tradition. Eliphaz positions himself within this authoritative stream and Job outside it.
15.19: “To those whose land was their own alone, before foreigners came among them.” This parenthesis may assert the purity of the tradition: it originated in a land uncorrupted by foreign influence. Some interpreters see it as a reference to pre-Israelite patriarchal tradition — the Edomite wisdom tradition, given that Eliphaz is a Temanite from Edom, a region renowned for wisdom (Jer 49:7; Obad 8). The tradition Eliphaz claims is ethnic and territorial, not merely professional. This grounds his authority claim in deep indigenous wisdom rather than mere learned craft.
15.20: “The wicked man twists in anguish all his days; the years allotted to the tyrant are numbered.” The fate-of-the-wicked poem begins. Mithôlēl (twisting in anguish, writhing) describes persistent torment — not a sudden catastrophe but a lifelong condition of inner suffering. ʿārîṣ (tyrant, ruthless man) — the word used for oppressors and violent rulers throughout the prophets (Isa 13:11; 25:3–5; Ps 37:35). Eliphaz’s portrait begins with existential anguish: the wicked man is not at peace even in apparent prosperity. This is psychologically astute even if theologically misapplied to Job.
15.21: “A sound of terror is always in his ears; in his moments of peace the destroyer strikes him.” Qôl-pᵉḥāḏîm bᵉʾoznāyw (a sound of terrors in his ears) — perpetual anxiety as the inner life of the wicked. Bišlômāh (in his peace/security) the destroyer comes — the word šālôm (peace, wholeness) normally denotes the condition of the righteous; for the wicked, even apparent peace contains the seed of terror. Šōḏēḏ (destroyer) — a term used for the divine agent of destruction (Jer 48:8; 51:56). The wicked man can never enjoy the peace that is the righteous man’s inheritance.
15.22: “He has no hope of escaping from the darkness; he is marked for the sword.” Lōʾ-yaʾᵃmên šûḇ (he does not trust/believe he can return) from the darkness — a permanent condition of hopelessness. The darkness that envelopes Job (10:21–22) is here attributed to the wicked. This is the first point of contact between Eliphaz’s description of the wicked and Job’s description of his own condition — a contact that makes the implicit application to Job increasingly clear as the poem develops. Ṣāpûn hûʾ liḥāreḇ (he is appointed for the sword) — his fate is sealed.
15.23: “He wanders in search of food, crying, ‘Where is it?’ He knows the day of darkness is ready and near.” The wandering hunger of the wicked contrasts with the settled prosperity of the righteous. Nāḏeḏ hûʾ lāleḥem ʾayyēh (he wanders for bread — where?) — the exile-and-hunger motif that in the prophets marks divine judgment (Lam 1:11; 4:9). The awareness of impending darkness is the psychological torment: not only does the day of darkness come, but the wicked person knows it is near. This consciousness of doom-without-escape is the distinctive signature of Eliphaz’s wicked man.
15.24: “Distress and anguish terrorize him; they overpower him like a king advancing to battle.” Ṣar ûmᵉṣûqāh (distress and anguish) — the classic paired terms of covenant curse (Deut 28:53,55,57; Isa 8:22; Zeph 1:15). The simile “like a king advancing to battle” (kᵉmelek ʿāṯîḏ lᵉkîḏôr) — the wicked person’s internal suffering overwhelms him like an invading army. This is the ancient Near Eastern war-metaphor for psychological/spiritual crisis. The comparison inverts normal power: the “great man” (by worldly measures) is overwhelmed by forces he cannot fight.
15.25: “For he has stretched out his hand against God and defied the Almighty.” The explicit sin of the wicked man: hand-stretching against God (nāṭāh ʿal-ʾēl yāḏô) and defiance (ʾel-šaddai yitgabbār). This is Eliphaz’s implicit charge against Job: Job’s speeches constitute exactly this — stretching the hand against God, acting with defiance against the Almighty. The escalation from the first-cycle speech (4:7–8) is evident: then Eliphaz hinted; now he charges. The rhetoric of the wicked man is designed to describe Job without naming him.
15.26: “Charging at him neck-forward, pressing against the thick boss of his shield.” The combat imagery intensifies: the wicked man charges God like a warrior (yārûṣ ʾēlāyw bᵉṣawwārô, charging at him neck-forward). Bᵉgaḇ bᵉginnôṯ māginnāyw (pressing against the thick bosses of his shields) — the bosses are the raised metal knobs on a warrior’s shield, the most armored part. Attacking the most armored part of God’s defense is the height of futile arrogance. The image participates in the OT warrior-God tradition (Exod 15; Hab 3; Ps 24:8) but here inverted: God is the defender against the wicked aggressor.
15.27: “He has become fat-faced, his sides padded with flesh.” The transition from warrior imagery to prosperity imagery: the wicked man is physically fat (kāśāh pānāyw bᵉḥêlpāyw, covered his face with his flesh) and wealthy-looking. In the ancient world, physical corpulence was a sign of prosperity (Ps 17:10; 73:4–7; Jer 5:28). The wicked man’s outward flourishing is the context for the destruction that follows: he looks prosperous, but he is building on a foundation that will collapse. This is the paradox of Psalm 73 and Proverbs 1–9 — the temporary prosperity of the wicked.
15.28: “He makes his home in ruined cities, in houses no one else inhabits.” The dwelling-in-ruins detail is striking: despite apparent prosperity (v27), the wicked man inhabits condemned places. This may reflect a judgment-tradition: the wicked are those who take possession of places under divine curse (compare the destruction of Sodom — Gen 19:24–25 — and prophetic descriptions of ruined cities as signs of divine judgment: Isa 13:20–22; Jer 50:39–40). The wicked man’s home is already under curse even when he occupies it; his prosperity is built on condemned ground.
15.29: “He will not remain wealthy; his substance will not last.” The prediction of reversal: current prosperity will not endure. Lōʾ-yeʿᵃšer (he will not be wealthy), lōʾ-yāqûm ḥêlô (his wealth will not endure). This is the foundational claim of the two-ways theology: eventual reversal of apparent prosperity. The wisdom literature consistently argues this case (Ps 37:1–2,9–10; 49:16–20; Prov 11:28). Eliphaz is applying to the wicked in general what he implies Job’s future will be if Job continues on his current path.
15.30: “He will not escape from the darkness; flame will wither his branches, and by the breath of God’s mouth he will be swept away.” Lōʾ-yāsûr miṇ-ḥōšeḵ (he will not turn from the darkness) — the darkness is permanent entrapment. Yibaššēš šalhaḇeṯ nîṣṣô (flame will wither his blossom/branch) — the tree imagery for human life: the wicked man’s flourishing branch is scorched. Wᵉyāsur bᵉrûaḥ pîhû (and he will be removed by the breath of God’s mouth) — the divine breath that gives life (33:4) here removes the wicked man like wind blows away chaff (Ps 1:4; Isa 17:13). Creation-breath becomes judgment-breath.
15.31: “Let him not deceive himself by trusting in what is empty, for emptiness will be his only return.” Šāwᵉ (emptiness, vanity, falsehood) as both the foundation the wicked trusts and the reward they receive — the boomerang principle of Proverbs (Prov 1:18: “they lie in wait for their own blood”). The verse may be a warning-instruction or a description: either “let him not deceive himself” (hortatory) or “he deceives himself” (descriptive). The ambiguity is itself meaningful: both the warning and its violation are present simultaneously in the wicked man’s situation.
15.32: “It will be completed before his time; his branch will not stay green.” Bᵉlōʾ-yômô (before his day/time) — premature death as divine judgment. The green branch (kappô ṯirʾᵃneh) that fails to remain green is the tree-of-life image inverted: instead of the righteous man’s flourishing (Ps 1:3; Prov 11:28: “the righteous will flourish like a green leaf”), the wicked man’s branch withers prematurely. The temporal element (before his time) responds to the problem of delayed justice that Job has raised: the wicked’s prosperity is always temporary even when it appears enduring.
15.33: “He will shed his unripe grapes like the vine, and drop his blossoms like the olive tree.” The agricultural metaphors: fruit production that fails before maturity. Yāḥmōs kāggepen bišrô (he will violently strip off his unripe grapes) — the ḥāmas (violence, stripping) is done to his own fruit; his violence destroys his own productivity. The olive blossom (nēṣeṯ zāyiṯ) that drops before bearing fruit is a standard image of failed potential (Deut 28:40). The wicked man’s descendants and projects are the “fruit” that never matures — a generational curse.
15.34: “For the assembly of the godless will be desolate, and fire will consume the tents of corruption.” ʿᵃḏaṯ ḥānēp (assembly of the godless/profane) — the community rather than just the individual. The fate-of-the-wicked judgment extends to the social group: the whole assembly will be desolate (galmûḏ, barren/desolate). Bribĕ šōḥaḏ (tents of bribery/corruption) — in the ANE, bribery and corruption were the emblematic sins of unjust rulers and judges. Fire as divine judgment on corrupt settlements recalls the fate of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24) and the prophetic judgment oracles (Amos 1–2).
15.35: “They conceive trouble and give birth to evil; their inmost being produces only deceit.” The reproductive metaphor for wickedness — hārāh ʿāmāl wᵉyāled ʾāwen (conceiving trouble and giving birth to iniquity) — recalls Ps 7:14 (“he who conceives trouble and gives birth to lies”) and Isa 59:4 (“they conceive trouble and give birth to evil”). The ḥarsô (their inner part/belly) that produces mirmāh (deceit) closes the poem with an inwardness claim: the wicked person’s core is generative of nothing but deception. The poem’s structural movement — from existential anguish (v20) to explicit rebellion (v25) to physical destruction (vv30–34) to moral inwardness (v35) — is deliberately comprehensive.

16.1: Job responds to Eliphaz’s second speech. The dialogic pattern of the book is at its most intense in the second cycle: the Friends attack more personally; Job’s responses become more anguished and more direct in their address to God. Chapter 16 is often considered one of the high points of the book’s poetry — the complaint against God as warrior (vv9–14) and the appeal to the heavenly witness (vv18–21) both appear here. The speech is also structured around the contrast between the Friends’ false comfort and the genuine witness Job seeks in heaven.
16.2: “I have heard all this before. You are miserable comforters, every one of you.” Mᵉnaḥămê ʿāmāl (comforters of trouble/toil) — the bitter reversal of the Friends’ stated purpose. They came to “comfort” (lᵉnaḥamô, 2:11) but have produced ʿāmāl (toil, grief, trouble).
