The term ʿāmāl encompasses both the labor of suffering and the grief it produces — the Friends have intensified Job’s suffering rather than relieving it. This is the paradigmatic indictment of theological counsel that prioritizes systematic correctness over genuine compassion with the sufferer.
16.3: “Will these empty words never stop? What gives you the confidence to keep answering me?” Diḇrê-rûaḥ (words of wind/breath) — the same accusation Eliphaz made against Job (15:2: hot east wind) is turned back on the Friends. Mah-yamrîṣᵉḵā kî taʿᵃneh (what provokes/emboldens you to keep answering?) — the verb mārēṣ (to strengthen, provoke, make bold) suggests that something external is driving the Friends’ persistence. Job cannot understand why they continue when their words provide no relief. The exhaustion of unproductive theological debate is palpable.
16.4: “I could talk just like you if you were in my place — I could pile words against you and shake my head at you.” Job performs the perspectival reversal that the Friends have systematically refused to do. Lûle ʾᵃnî kᵉnafšᵉḵem (if I were in your place) — the empathy exercise the Friends did for seven days (2:13) but abandoned when they began to speak. The head-shaking (ʾānîṭāh ʿᵃlêḵem bᵉrōʾšî) is the gesture of contemptuous pity (Ps 22:7; Jer 18:16; Lam 2:15) — the same gesture Job endures. He is imagining delivering to them what they are delivering to him.
16.5: “I would strengthen you with my words, and the comfort of my lips would ease your grief.” The counterfactual comfort Job would offer — genuine strengthening (ʾᵃḥazzēq bᵉḵem bᵉmî pî) rather than accusation. The verse is both accusatory (the Friends’ comfort fails this standard) and self-revelatory (Job understands what genuine comfort looks like). The comfort of the lips (nîḏ śᵉpāṯay yaḥśôḵ) that restrains grief is the wisdom literature’s ideal of therapeutic speech (Prov 15:4; 12:25). Job has internalized the wisdom ideal; the Friends have violated it.
16.6: “If I speak, the pain is not relieved; and if I stay silent, how much of it leaves me?” The double bind of unrelievable suffering: speech brings no relief (lōʾ-yeḥaś kᵉʾēḇî), but silence relieves none of it either (mah-minnî yaʿᵃḇōr). This existential paralysis — no action available that would improve the situation — is what distinguishes Job’s suffering from ordinary trial. It is also the theological problem at the center of the book: if there is no action Job can take that would change his situation, then either his suffering is beyond human remedy (which it is) or God must act directly (which Job demands).
16.7: “But now he has utterly exhausted me; he has made desolate everyone around me.” Job shifts from the Friends to God as the source of his desolation. ʿAttāh hilʾāh (now he has exhausted/made weary) — lāʾāh means to be worn out, exhausted to the point of breakdown. The desolation of Job’s community (kol-ʿāḏāṯî) — everyone around him is also affected. Job’s suffering is not merely individual; it has shattered his social world. Compare 19:13–19 where Job catalogs the social alienation in detail. The corporate dimension of the suffering is part of what makes it appear like divine curse rather than mere tragedy.
16.8: “You have wasted my flesh until it shows — that itself is a witness; my emaciation rises up and testifies against me to my face.” Job’s body has become evidence — but evidence of the opposite of what the Friends claim. They argue his body-as-ruin proves his guilt; Job says his body-as-ruin is the witness to his unjust treatment. Wᵉtiqmᵉṭēnî lᵉʿēḏ (it has become a witness against me) — the physical wreckage of Job’s body testifies, but to what? The ambiguity is deliberate: the same visible evidence supports opposite verdicts depending on the interpretive framework. Job’s framework reads the suffering as testimony against God’s justice; the Friends’ framework reads it as testimony against Job’s righteousness.
16.9: “He has torn me apart in his wrath and pursued me; he gnashes his teeth at me; my enemy fixes his sharpened gaze upon me.” The divine-warrior attack imagery begins in earnest. ʾAppô ṭārap wayiśṭᵉmēnî (in his anger he has torn and hated/pursued me) — ṭārap is the verb used for predatory animals tearing prey (Gen 37:33; 44:28; Hos 6:1). The teeth-gnashing (yiḥᵃrōq ʿālay bᵉšinnāyw) and the enemy-gaze (ṣārî yilṭôš ʿênāyw lî) are the gestures of predator toward prey. Job has reversed the creation-order: instead of the divine Creator caring for the creature, the divine Warrior hunts it. This language of God-as-predator is the most extreme form of the book’s theodicy-challenge and has direct parallels in Lamentations 3:1–18.
16.10: “They open their mouths wide at me; they strike my cheek in contempt; they have gathered themselves together against me.” The human mockers join the divine assault: pāʿᵃrû ʿālay bᵉpîhem (they gape at me with their mouths) — the gesture of contemptuous mockery (Ps 22:13; Lam 2:16; 3:46). The cheek-strike (lᵉḥît yakkûnî bᵉḥerpāh) is a gesture of profound humiliation in the ancient Near East (1 Kgs 22:24; Mic 5:1; Isa 50:6). The convergence of divine assault (v9) and human humiliation (v10) creates the double abandonment — by God and by human community simultaneously — that will culminate in 19:13–22.
16.11: “God has surrendered me to the evildoers and thrown me into the hands of the wicked.” Yasgîrēnî ʾēl ʾel-ʿᵃwîl (God has shut me up/surrendered me to the ungodly) — sāgar (to shut up, deliver up) is the legal term for extradition or surrender to an enemy (Deut 32:30; 1 Sam 23:11; Ps 31:9). God is the agent who has delivered Job to his enemies rather than protecting him from them. This is the paradox of the prologue reversed from below: the reader knows God permitted Satan to afflict Job; Job experiences it as God handing him over to the wicked. Both perspectives are simultaneously true, which is part of the book’s theological depth.
16.12: “I was living in ease, and he shattered me; he grabbed me by the neck and broke me apart; he stood me up as his target.” Šālēw hāyîtî wayyᵉparpᵉrēnî (I was at ease and he shattered me) — šālēw (quiet, at rest, at ease) is Job’s pre-disaster condition; pārpar (to shake apart, shatter) is its violent reversal. The neck-seizure (wayyōʾḥᵃzēnî bᵉʿōrep) and being set up as target (wayyᵉśîmēnî lô lᵉmatarrāh) — matarrāh (target) appears only here and in Lam 3:12 (“he bent his bow and set me as a target for his arrow”). Lamentations draws on Job’s vocabulary, suggesting that the exilic community’s suffering was interpreted through the Job-template.
16.13: “His archers surround me; he slashes into my kidneys without mercy and spills my gall on the ground.” Rabbāyw yibbāqᵃʿû kᵉlāyōṯāy (his archers/arrows break through my kidneys) — the kidneys (kᵉlāyôṯ) in ancient Near Eastern anatomy were the seat of conscience and deepest self (Ps 7:10; 16:7; Jer 11:20; 17:10). To pierce the kidneys is to penetrate to the innermost person. Yišpōḵ lāʾāreṣ mᵉrērāṯî (he pours out my gall on the ground) — the gall bladder spilling is a clinical description of catastrophic internal injury. The combination of military imagery (archers, breaching) and anatomical detail produces an image of total devastation of the interior life.
16.14: “He breaks through me, breach after breach; he charges at me like a great warrior.” Yiprᵉṣēnî pereṣ ʿal-pᵉnê-pāreṣ (he breaks through me, breach upon breach) — the pāreṣ (breach) imagery from siege warfare: a wall breached multiple times cannot be repaired. Yārûṣ ʿālay kᵉgibbôr (he charges at me like a warrior) — the gibbôr (mighty man, hero) was the elite warrior of ancient armies. God assaulting Job like a siege-engineer breaching walls and a soldier charging is the culmination of the warrior imagery in vv9–14. The passage as a whole is one of the most powerful descriptions of divine assault in the OT and is echoed structurally by Lamentations 3.
16.15: “I have sewn sackcloth over my skin and pressed my dignity down into the dust.” Job’s outward mourning-posture matches his inward devastation. Tāpārtî śaq ʿᵃlê giltî (I sewed sackcloth over my skin) — sackcloth (śaq) was the standard garment of mourning, grief, and petition (Gen 37:34; Joel 1:8,13; Jonah 3:6–8). Wᵉʿôlaltî bāʿāpār qarnî (and I have thrust my horn into the dust) — the horn (qeren) was the symbol of dignity, strength, and honor in the ancient world (1 Sam 2:1,10; Ps 75:4–5; 92:10; 148:14). Pressing the horn into the dust is the act of complete self-abasement, the reversal of honored status.
16.16: “My face is flushed raw from weeping, and the shadow of death rests on my eyelids.” Pānay ḥᵃmarmarāh minnî-ḇeḵî (my face is red/inflamed from weeping) — the physical marks of prolonged grief. Ṣalmāweṯ ʿal-ʿappappāy (shadow of death on my eyelids) — ṣalmāweṯ appears again, now not as the place of death but as a presence settled on Job’s face. The eyelids as the location of ṣalmāweṯ is striking: the organ of sight is covered by death-shadow. Job’s vision is clouded by proximity to death. Despite this, v17 immediately states his innocence — the external marks of death-sentence do not correspond to interior guilt.
16.17: “Yet no violence has been done by my hands, and my prayer has been pure.” The contrastive ʿal lōʾ-ḥāmās bᵉkappāy (yet no violence in my palms) — the palms as the locus of moral action in the ancient world. Ḥāmās (violence, wrong) is one of the strongest terms for unjust oppression in the OT (Gen 6:11; Ezek 28:16; Hab 1:2–3). ûṣᵉlāṯî zakāh (and my prayer has been pure/clear) — the purity of prayer reflects the purity of moral standing. This verse is the hinge between the warrior-assault passage (vv9–16) and the witness-appeal (vv18–21): the suffering described in vv9–16 is unjust precisely because of the innocence stated in v17.
16.18: “O earth, do not cover my blood; let my cry find no place to settle!” The appeal to the earth as witness-for-the-prosecution is grounded in the Abel tradition: Abel’s blood cried out from the ground where it was covered (Gen 4:10–11). Uncovered blood in the ancient world was a legal problem: it demanded justice and could not rest until satisfied (Num 35:33; Isa 26:21; Ezek 24:7–8). By appealing for his blood to remain uncovered (ʾal-tᵉḵasî dāmî ʾāreṣ), Job is inviting the earth itself to carry his cry for justice upward until it is answered. The earth-as-witness sets up the heaven-as-witness in v19.
16.19: “Even now my witness is in heaven, and my advocate is on high.” ʿēḏî baššāmayim (my witness in the heavens) and śāhᵃḏî bammᵉrômîm (my testifier on high) — this is the most significant intercessor-passage in the second cycle. Who is this heavenly witness? Interpretive options: (1) God himself — a split between God the antagonist (vv9–14) and God the advocate; (2) an angelic figure in the divine council parallel to the meliṣ of 33:23; (3) Job’s own righteousness personified as a heavenly witness. The most natural reading in context is that Job is appealing to God against God — a desperate appeal to God’s own integrity to vindicate what God’s actions seem to deny. This will reach its climax in 19:25–27 (the gōʾēl).
16.20: “My friends scorn me, yet my eye pours out tears to God.” The social abandonment — friends who mock (mᵉlîṣay rēʿāy, my intercessors/friends are my scorners) — against the persistent upward orientation of weeping to God. ʾEl-ʾĕlôah (to God) — the tears are directional; they go not downward into the earth but upward toward God. The paradox: God is both the cause of the suffering (vv9–14) and the address of the tears (v20). This is not contradiction but the structure of biblical lament: the complaint is directed to the very One who is accused, because there is no other court of appeal.
16.21: “If only a mortal man could plead with God the way a man pleads for his neighbor.” The môkîaḥ-longing from 9:33 returns in different form: wᵉyôkîaḥ lᵉgeḇer ʿim-ʾĕlôah ûḇen-ʾāḏām lᵉrêʿēhû (if a man could argue with God as a man argues for his neighbor). The legal advocacy image (yākaḥ, to argue/contend in legal setting) applied to the impossible case: a human being pleading another human’s case before God. Job longs for this structure to exist between humanity and God. The NT answer is the incarnate High Priest who was in every way like his brothers (Heb 2:17–18; 4:15–16): one who can plead humanity’s case from within the human condition.
16.22: “For the few years left are slipping past, and I am walking a road from which there is no return.” Šᵉnôṯ mispar yᵉʾĕṯāyû (the years of number/few years will come) — Job’s awareness that time is running out for the vindication he seeks. Wᵉʾōraḥ lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ ʾehlōḵ (and I am walking a path of no return) — the one-way road to death makes the urgency of justice acute. If vindication does not come before death, it will not come at all — unless there is something beyond death (ch19 will explore this). The speech ends with the one-way road, preparing for ch17’s meditation on Sheol as the only destination.

17.1: Job continues his response (ch17 may be part of a single speech begun in ch16, divided by modern chapter divisions). Rûḥî ḥubbālāh (my spirit is ruined/exhausted) — ḥābal means to bind, pledge, or destroy; here it suggests the spirit has been handed over to destruction. Yamay nizᵃʿākû (my days are extinguished) — the lamp-imagery for life (cf. 18:5–6): Job’s days are being put out like a lamp. Qᵉḇārîm lî (the grave for me) — the plural may indicate multiple graves awaiting, or is an intensified form. Death has become Job’s horizon.
17.2: “Surely there are mockers all around me, and my eye rests on their hostile provocation.” Hᵃtullîm (mockers) surround Job; their mockery is not background noise but foreground torment. Wᵉḇᵉmirāhôṯam tālān ʿênî (my eye rests on their provocation) — the eye that was pouring out tears to God (16:20) now rests on human hostility. This contrast between vertical lament (toward God) and horizontal hostility (from people) structures much of Job’s situation. The same tension appears in Psalm 88 — the most hopeless of the psalms — where the sufferer’s companions become darkness itself.
17.3: “Give me a pledge yourself; who else is there to strike hands with me?” Śîmāh-nāʾ ʿārᵉḇēnî ʿimmāḵ (place a pledge-surety with yourself for me) — the legal concept of ʿēreḇ (surety, pledge). In ancient Near Eastern commerce and law, a surety was a third party who guaranteed another’s debt or obligation (Gen 43:9: Judah as surety for Benjamin; Prov 6:1–5; 11:15; 17:18). Job asks God to become his own guarantee — a logical paradox: God pledges to God for Job. The striking-of-hands (mî hûʾ lî yitqāʿ, who will shake hands for me?) was the gesture sealing a surety agreement. Hebrews 7:22 interprets Christ’s priesthood in exactly these terms: “Jesus has become the guarantee of a better covenant.”
17.4: “For you have closed off their minds from true understanding; therefore you will not let them triumph.” Libbām ṣāpantā mibināh (their heart you have hidden from understanding) — God has concealed understanding from the Friends, which is why they cannot help Job. This is a striking theological claim: the Friends’ inadequacy is not merely intellectual failure but divine action. Their hearts are closed to the understanding that would allow them to serve as genuine advocates. ʿal-kēn lōʾ tᵉrōmēm (therefore you will not let them triumph/be exalted) — the Friends’ inability to vindicate themselves before God is connected to their inability to understand Job’s situation. Divine hardening serves the book’s design: no human wisdom can solve Job’s problem; only God can.
17.5: “He who betrays his friends for personal gain — his children’s eyes will fail.” This proverbial statement is applied to the Friends: they have betrayed Job (who is their rēaʿ, friend/companion) by choosing comfortable theology over genuine solidarity. Lᵉḥēleq (for a share/portion) — they have a stake in maintaining the retributive system because they benefit from a world where righteousness guarantees prosperity. Their children’s failing eyes (wᵉʿênê bānāyw tiklenāh) is the curse-formula: the sin of the fathers visits the third and fourth generation (Exod 20:5). The Friends’ theological betrayal of Job is functionally equivalent to betraying a friend for personal gain.
17.6: “He has turned me into a proverb among the nations — I am one before whose face men spit.” Wᵉhiṣṣîḡanî limšōl ʿammîm (he has set me up as a proverb/byword of the peoples) — being made a māšāl (proverb, taunt-song, byword) is a standard formula of covenant curse in the OT (Deut 28:37; 1 Kgs 9:7; Jer 24:9; Ezek 14:8; Ps 44:15). To become a byword is to become the negative example by which others measure their own fortunate condition. The face-spitting (tōpeṯ-pānîm ʾehyeh) is the extreme humiliation gesture (Num 12:14; Deut 25:9; Isa 50:6). Job has descended from honored elder (29:7–25) to social outcast.
17.7: “My eye has grown dim from sorrow, and all my limbs are like shadows.” Wattikah mikkāʿas ʿênî (my eye has grown dim/exhausted from vexation) — the eye that weeps (16:20), that rests on mockery (17:2), now fails from grief. Yᵉṣārāy kāṣṣēl kullām (my limbs are all like a shadow) — the bodily dissolution that began with flesh-wasting (16:8) reaches the entire body. In the ancient world, the shadow (ṣēl) was associated with both the insubstantial (Ps 39:6: “man is a mere shadow”; Eccl 6:12) and with protection (Ps 17:8; 91:1). Job has become the shadow without protection — insubstantial, about to disappear.
17.8: “Upright men are horrified by this; the innocent rises up against the godless.” A seemingly intrusive verse: the upright men’s horror may be at the injustice of Job’s treatment, or at Job’s apparent descent into impiety. The verse may be Job’s self-location within the community of the righteous: even if the Friends reject him, the yᵉšārîm (upright) and nāqî (innocent) can recognize the injustice. Wᵉṯāqōm ʿal-ḥānēp ḥônēn (the innocent rises against the godless) — Job positioning himself as the innocent who stands against the godless accusers, however the Friends have positioned themselves.
17.9: “The righteous man keeps to his course, and the one with clean hands grows stronger and stronger.” Despite everything, Job affirms the path of the righteous: wᵉyōsēp ṣaddîq darkô (the righteous man adds to/continues his way) and ûṭᵉhar-yāḏayim yōsîp ʾōmeṣ (the one with clean hands grows stronger). This is an affirmation of moral persistence against circumstantial assault — the righteous do not abandon their course because suffering contradicts their expectation. The verse anticipates the resolution of ch31, where Job maintains his integrity claim to the end, and stands in tension with the despair of vv1–7 and 11–16 within the same speech.
17.10: “But come, all of you, try again — I still cannot find a wise man among you.” The challenge to renewed debate: ûlām kullām tāšuḇû-nāʾ (but all of you, please return/try again). Despite the impossibility (“I still cannot find a wise man among you”), Job is not finished. This is the most direct assessment of the Friends’ failure: despite their appeals to tradition, age, and experience, none of them qualifies as a genuine ḥāḵām (wise man) in Job’s assessment. The wisdom tradition’s highest credential — being truly wise — is denied to its self-appointed representatives.
17.11: “My days are over; my plans have been cut off — even the secret longings of my heart.” Yāmay ʿāḇᵃrû zimōṯay (my days have passed, my purposes/plans) — zimmāh (plan, purpose, thought) here has the positive sense of life-plans and designs, not its common negative sense (wickedness). The cutting off of the heart’s possessions (môrāšê lᵉḇāḇî, the possessions of my heart) — the private longings that never became public — represents the most intimate loss: not just status and family but the unlived hopes that sustained his interior life.
17.12: “They make night into day; they say, ‘The light is near,’ even while darkness surrounds them.” The Friends’ false comfort: laylāh lᵉyôm yāśîmû (they set night as day) — inverting the order of reality to offer false hope. The optimistic declaration “light is near” (ʾôr qārôḇ mippᵉnê-ḥōšeḵ) in the presence of darkness is the definition of Eliphaz’s consolations (15:11): they claim imminent relief for someone surrounded by darkness. Job refuses this inversion: the darkness he experiences is real; naming it “light” is a lie. This epistemological conflict — reality versus comfortable optimism — is one of the book’s central concerns.
17.13: “If I wait, Sheol is my home — I have made my bed in the darkness.” Job’s expectation of where hope leads: not to light (v12, the Friends’ false promise) but to Sheol. Im-ʾᵃqawweh šᵉʾôl bêṯî (if I hope/wait, Sheol is my house) — waiting produces only Sheol as dwelling place. Bᵃḥōšeḵ rāpaʿdtî yᵉṣûʿî (in darkness I have spread my bed) — the bed-in-darkness is the most intimate image of dwelling: Job has not just passed through darkness but has made it his permanent resting place. The domestic language (house, bed) applied to Sheol is the extreme form of hopelessness: death has become home.
17.14: “I call out to corruption, ‘You are my father,’ and to the worm, ‘My mother and my sister.’” The grotesque reversal of covenant-family language: lašṣaḥaṯ qārāʾtî ʾāḇî ʾāttāh (to the pit/corruption I call: you are my father) and lārimmāh ʾimmi wᵉʾᵃḥōṯî (to the worm: my mother and my sister). The intimate family terms (ʾāḇ, father; ʾēm, mother; ʾāḥôṯ, sister) applied to decay and worms is the ultimate estrangement: the human relational network that was Job’s identity (1:1–5) has been replaced by the relational network of dissolution.
17.15: “Where then is my hope? Who can see hope for me?” Wᵉʾayyēh ʾēpô tiqwāṯî (and where then is my hope?) — tiqwāh (hope) is one of the book’s central terms. In ch14, Job explored hope under the image of the tree that might sprout (14:7–9) and concluded that, unlike the tree, human death offers no revival. Here the question is even starker: hope itself has no location. The question ûtiqwāṯî mî yᵉšûrennāh (who can see my hope?) suggests hope has become invisible — not just deferred but undetectable. The rhetorical despair here sets the stage for ch19’s unexpected eruption of hope in the gōʾēl passage.
17.16: “Will it descend with me to the gates of Sheol? Together we shall go down into the dust.” Baḏḏê šᵉʾôl tēreḏnāh (will they descend to the bars/gates of Sheol?) — the bars (badîm) or gates of Sheol are the portals of the underworld from which no return is possible (Isa 38:10: Hezekiah’s lament; Ps 9:13). Hope descends into the dust alongside Job — hope itself dies. This is the nadir of the speech and among the lowest points of the book before the gōʾēl passage of 19:25–27 reverses direction. The structural movement of the book requires this depth: the resolution that comes must be proportionate to the despair expressed.

18.1: Bildad delivers his second speech (his first was in ch8). Like Eliphaz’s second speech, it is more aggressive than the first. Where Bildad’s first speech appealed to natural and generational cycles (8:8–19), his second speech constructs an elaborate fate-of-the-wicked poem (vv5–21). It is the most sustained and vivid poetic treatment of the wicked man’s doom in the dialogue section, drawing on a range of ANE literary conventions: hunting-trap imagery, lamp-extinction symbolism, death-personification, and name-erasure curses.
18.2: “How long before you stop talking? Think first, and then we will have our say.” ʿaḏ-ʾānāh tiśîmûn qinṣê-millîn (how long will you set snares of words?) — the plural tiśîmûn suggests Bildad may be addressing Job and his supporters, or addressing the company collectively. Tāḇînû (understand/discern first) before speaking — the wisdom-pedagogy principle inverted: Bildad accuses Job of speaking without prior understanding. The two-step (ûḇaʿᵃḵēn nᵉḏabbēr, then we will speak) implies a proper order: comprehension before articulation. Bildad is positioning his own speech as properly understood, in contrast to Job’s reflexive protest.
18.3: “Why do you regard us as animals? Why are we worthless in your eyes?” Bildad’s personal pique: Job’s dismissal of the Friends as animals (nibᵉḏamennû, as animals) and as defiled/worthless (ṭāmîm bᵉʿênêḵem). Job has in fact compared himself favorably to the Friends’ wisdom (12:7–9: the animals and birds know more than the Friends know), which Bildad apparently took personally. The breakdown of the discourse community — from respectful debate to mutual contempt — is tracked in these accusation-counter-accusation exchanges that mark the second cycle.
18.4: “You who tear yourself apart in your rage — will the earth be forsaken on your account? Will the rock be moved from its place for you?” Ṭōrēp napšô bᵉʾappô (you who tear your own soul in your anger) — Bildad turns Job’s warrior-God imagery back on Job himself: the tearing is self-inflicted, the product of Job’s own rage. The rhetorical questions — will the earth be abandoned on your account? will the rock be moved for you? — mockingly suggest that Job believes the entire natural order should accommodate his complaints. The rock (ṣûr) as foundational stability recalls the creation theology in which God set the foundations immovably (38:4–7).
18.5: “Yes, the light of the wicked is extinguished; the flame of his fire gives no light.” The lamp-extinction motif begins the fate-of-the-wicked poem. Nēr rᵉšāʿîm yidʿāḵ (the lamp of the wicked is extinguished) — in the ancient world, the lamp (nēr) was both a practical light source and a symbol of life, family continuity, and the presence of the divine. To have one’s lamp extinguished was to have one’s life and line ended (Prov 13:9: “the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished”; 20:20; 24:20). The flame that gives no light (šabaḥ ʾēšô nōgāh) — even what appears to burn is without illuminating effect.
18.6: “The light in his tent grows dark, and the lamp over him is extinguished.” The movement from communal lamp (v5) to personal/domestic lamp (v6): ʾôr ḥāšaḵ bᵉʾōhᵃlô (the light darkens in his tent) and wᵉnērô ʿālāyw yiddaʿᵃḵ (his lamp above him is extinguished). The tent (ʾōhel) and the lamp-above-him are domestic images. In patriarchal and nomadic contexts, the well-lit tent was a sign of hospitality, prosperity, and protection; the dark tent is its opposite — abandonment and curse. The imagery applies to Job’s situation ironically: Job’s household has already been darkened (1:18–19).
18.7: “His powerful stride is shortened, and his own schemes bring him down.” Yiṣṣᵃrû ṣᵃʿᵃdê ʾônô (the steps of his strength are shortened) — the diminishment of physical power. Wᵉtašlîḵēhû ʿᵃṣāṯô (his own counsel/plans throw him down) — the self-entrapment motif: the wicked man’s downfall is engineered by his own scheming. This is the boomerang principle of Proverbs (Prov 1:18–19; 26:27; Ps 7:15–16): the trap the wicked set for others catches themselves. The transition from lamp imagery to stride-shortening to self-entrapment prepares the extensive trap-and-net imagery of vv8–10.
18.8: “He is thrown into a net by his own feet, and he walks right onto a snare.” Kî šullaḥ bᵉreṯeṯ bᵉraglāyw (for he is sent into a net by his own feet) — the passive suggests divine agency behind the snare. The extensive trap vocabulary in vv8–10 — rešeṯ (net), śᵉḇāḵāh (snare), pāḥ (trap), ṣāmîm (snare), ḥeḇel (rope/cord) — is a deliberate accumulation of hunting imagery. In the ANE, hunting provided one of the primary metaphors for divine judgment (Ezek 12:13; 17:20; Jer 18:22; Hos 7:12). The multiplication of trap-types implies inescapability: however the wicked man moves, a trap awaits.
18.9: “A trap catches him by the heel; a snare holds him tight.” Yōʾḥez bᵉʿāqēḇ pāḥ (a trap seizes him by the heel) — the heel (ʿāqēḇ) as the point of vulnerability. The heel is the anatomically vulnerable site in ancient combat and is associated in the OT with Jacob/Israel (Gen 25:26: grasping the heel) and with the serpent’s attack (Gen 3:15: striking the heel). Yᵃḥᵃzēq ʿālāyw ṣāmîm (the snare holds him firm) — the trap’s grip is total. The hunting imagery in vv8–10 may have influenced Psalm 91:3 (“he will save you from the fowler’s snare”) as the positive counter-promise.
18.10: “A rope is buried in the ground for him, and a trap waits on his path.” Ṭāmûn bāʾāreṣ ḥaḇlô (his rope is hidden in the ground) — the concealed trap, the hidden danger. The trap waits on the nᵉṯîḇāh (path, way) — the path-language ties the hunting imagery to the wisdom literature’s two-ways theology: the wicked man’s very path is mined with traps. Compare Ps 1:6 (“the way of the wicked will perish”) — not through external intervention alone but through the structural entrapment that the wicked way entails. The “way” that leads to death is a trapped way.
18.11: “Terrors frighten him on every side and push him forward at every step.” Sābîḇ bittᵃhûhû bālāhôṯ (terrors frighten him on every side) — the plural bālāhôṯ (terrors, sudden destructions) are personified forces surrounding the wicked man. Wᵉhēpîṣuhû lᵉraglāyw (they push/scatter him at his feet) — the terror-forces drive him forward, not allowing him to stand. The image is of a panicked animal driven by beaters on all sides — no matter which direction the wicked man moves, terror drives him further into the trap. The “king of terrors” (v14) is the destination toward which these surrounding terrors drive him.
18.12: “His strength is starved, and disaster stands ready at his side.” Yᵉhî-rāʿēb ʾônô (his strength is hungry/starved) — the strength (ʾôn) that made his stride powerful (v7) is now famished. Wᵉʾêḏ nāḵôn lᵉṣalʿô (and disaster stands ready at his side) — ʾêḏ (disaster, calamity) as a quasi-personified attendant: not a random misfortune but a constant companion ready to strike. The image of disaster as a constant companion at the side contrasts with Psalm 23:6 (“goodness and steadfast love will follow me all my days”) — for the wicked, the constant follower is ʾêḏ rather than ḥeseḏ.
18.13: “His skin is eaten away piece by piece; the firstborn of death devours his body.” Yōʾkal baḏḏê ʿôrô (it eats his skin piece by piece) — a disease that consumes the body from outside. Yōʾkal baḏḏāyw bᵉḵôr māweṯ (the firstborn of death devours his limbs) — bᵉḵôr māweṯ (firstborn of death) is unique in the OT: a metaphorical or mythological figure, the most powerful of death’s offspring. In Canaanite mythology, Mot (Death) was a deity with a household and realm; his “firstborn” would be his most powerful offspring. The verse applies to Job’s condition exactly — his skin has been consumed (2:7: “painful sores from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head”).
18.14: “His confidence is ripped away from his home, and he is brought before the king of terrors.” Yinnāteq mēʾōhᵃlô miḇṭaḥô (his confidence is torn/ripped from his tent) — miḇṭāḥ (confidence, security, what one trusts in) is what Eliphaz said was available to the righteous (4:6) but the wicked lose. Wᵉtaṣʿidēhû lᵉmelek ballāhôṯ (and he is marched before the king of terrors) — melek ballāhôṯ (king of terrors) is the most vivid death-personification in the book. In Ugaritic texts, Mot is a royal figure who rules the underworld. The formal march (tṣʿd, to cause to march/step) before this king is a court procession — the wicked man is brought as a condemned prisoner before death’s throne. Revelation 1:18 responds: “I hold the keys of Death and Hades.”
18.15: “Something alien has moved into his tent; brimstone is scattered over his home.” Tiškon bᵉʾōhᵃlô mibbᵉlî-lô (something dwells in his tent that is not his) — the tent occupied by an alien presence, the opposite of the prosperous household. Wᵉzōreh ʿal-nāwêhû goprit (brimstone is scattered over his dwelling) — brimstone (goprit) is the material of divine judgment: the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24), the judgment oracles of the prophets (Isa 34:9; Ezek 38:22; Ps 11:6). The scattering of brimstone over the dwelling makes the site permanently consecrated to destruction — nothing will grow there again (Deut 29:23).
18.16: “His roots dry up below; his branches wither above.” The arboreal metaphor for human life and posterity: mittaḥaṯ šāreśāyw yiḇāšû (his roots dry up below) and ûmimmaʿal yimmāl qᵉṣîrô (his branches/harvest wither above). Job used the same tree image to express hope (14:7–9: “there is hope for a tree — if it is cut down it will sprout again”). Bildad denies that hope for the wicked: the roots fail before the branches wither, meaning there is no possibility of recovery. The comprehensive destruction — roots and branches — eliminates both past (roots) and future (branches) simultaneously.
18.17: “His memory vanishes from the land; he has no name in the open places.” Ziḵrô ʾāḇaḏ min-ʾāreṣ (his memory perishes from the earth) and wᵉlōʾ-šēm lô ʿal-pᵉnê-ḥûṣ (no name for him in the open/outside) — the erasure of name and memory is the ultimate death in the ancient world, where the name’s survival constituted a form of immortality. The name that persisted in the public places (ḥûṣ = streets, public squares) was the sign of a life that had mattered. Proverbs 10:7 and 22:1 make the name’s persistence a mark of the righteous. To have no name in the open places is the final deletion from communal memory.
18.18: “He is driven from light into darkness and banished from the world.” Yihdᵃpēhû miʾôr ʾel-ḥōšeḵ (he is thrust from light into darkness) — the light-darkness contrast that runs through the book reaches its destination here: the wicked man arrives at the permanent darkness that was his trajectory from 15:22. Ûmittēḇēl yᵉniddūhû (and from the world he is banished) — tēḇēl (world, inhabited earth) is the sphere of human community and divine ordering. Banishment from the tēḇēl is cosmic exile — not just social death or personal death but exclusion from the ordered world itself. Compare Cain’s banishment (Gen 4:12–14) as the prototype of this condition.
18.19: “He has no offspring or descendants among his people, and no survivor where he once lived.” Lōʾ-nîn lô wᵉlōʾ-neḵeḏ bᵉʿammô (no son and no grandson among his people) — the genealogical erasure following the name-erasure of v17. Wᵉʾên śārîḏ bimgûrāyw (no survivor in his dwelling places) — the word śārîḏ (survivor, refugee) was used of those who survived divine judgment (Jer 42:17; 44:14; Joel 2:32). The complete absence of any survivor from the wicked man’s household is the absolute form of the divine judgment that leaves nothing remaining. Bildad’s description of total annihilation is implicitly applied to Job: no children (1:18–19), no household (1:15–17).
18.20: “Those who come after him are horrified by his fate; those who went before him are seized with dread.” ʿal-yômô nāšammû ʾaḥᵃrōnîm (at his day those who come after are horrified) and wᵉqaḏmōnîm ʾāḥᵃzû śāʿar (and those who went before are seized with dread) — the judgment functions as a warning to all generations: past and future witnesses are both appalled. This universalizing gesture frames the fate-of-the-wicked poem as an instructional text for all observers. The pedagogical function of observed judgment is a key mechanism of wisdom ethics (Prov 24:30–34; Ps 37:35–36).
18.21: Bildad’s summary verdict closes the poem with the explicit category: lōʾ-yāḏaʿ ʾēl (the one who does not know God). In the wisdom and prophetic traditions, “not knowing God” is the fundamental orientation that produces all the downstream consequences the poem has described (Jer 2:8; 4:22; 9:3,6; Hos 4:1,6). Not-knowing is both cognitive (lacking accurate understanding of who God is) and relational (refusing the covenant-relationship with God). Bildad’s implicit accusation: Job’s conduct reveals him to be one who does not know God — the exact opposite of the book’s opening claim (1:1: “he feared God”).

27.1: 'And Job again took up his discourse' — wayyōsep̄ ʾîyyôḇ śēʾt mĕšālô. The word māšāl (discourse/parable/proverb) is the formal wisdom-literature genre marker: it governs the wisdom sayings of Proverbs (1:1; 10:1; 25:1) and the longer discourses in Job. By designating his speech a māšāl, Job claims the authoritative form of the wisdom sage. The 'again' (wayyōsep̄ = he continued/repeated) may mark the resumption after a gap — ch26 ended Job's response to Bildad, and ch27 begins a new section that will extend through ch31. The structural position: this opens Job's final extended defense (chs. 27-31), the climactic self-assessment before the divine speech.
27.2: Job swears an oath 'as God lives' (ḥay-ʾēl) despite God being the very one who has 'denied him justice' (hēsîr mišpāṭî) and 'made him bitter' (hēmar nap̄šî). This is one of the most theologically striking moves in the book: Job invokes the name of his adversary as the ground of his oath. In the OT, oath-formulas 'as YHWH lives' (ḥay-YHWH) are standard judicial forms affirming commitment to truth (1 Sam 14:39,45; Ruth 3:13; Jer 4:2). Job continues to use God as the ground of truth-telling even when he experiences God as his opponent — preserving theological integrity while maintaining legal complaint.
27.3: 'As long as my breath (nišmāṯî) remains and God's breath (rûaḥ ʾĕlôah) is in my nostrils' — the verse identifies two aspects of Job's life-breath: his own nišmāh and the divine rûaḥ. Both terms connect to Gen 2:7 where God breathed the 'nišmaṯ ḥayyîm' into the creature. The double reference implies that even Job's physical survival is sustained by divine action — the very God he disputes is the one keeping him alive to make the dispute. This creates the paradox of theodicy: life as both the gift that enables complaint and the affliction that motivates it.
27.4: 'My lips will not speak wickedness, and my tongue will not utter deception' — the negative oath formula of the wisdom tradition: Ps 34:13 ('keep your tongue from evil and your lips from deceitful speech'); Ps 141:3 ('set a guard over my mouth'); Prov 8:7-8 (Wisdom's lips speak truth without deceit). The double denial (lips/tongue in parallel) applies the standard body-member rhetoric of Psalms and Proverbs: the two speech organs together represent the totality of verbal expression. Job is claiming pre-emptive fidelity to the wisdom standards he knows the friends expect.
27.5: 'God forbid that I should agree you are right' (ḥālîlāh lî ʾim-ʾaṣdîq ʾetḵem) — ḥālîlāh is the strongest negative imprecation in Hebrew (literally 'profanation to me if'; translated in KJV as 'God forbid'); it expresses utter repudiation. 'Until I die I will not surrender my integrity' (ʿaḏ-ʾegwaʿ lōʾ-ʾāsîr tummāṯî mimmennî) — tummāh (integrity/wholeness, the character noun from tām; cf. 1:1,8 where God describes Job as 'blameless'/tām). Job's integrity is not just a moral quality but his theological identity: surrendering it would mean agreeing to a false confession, which would be the greater wickedness.
27.6: 'I hold fast to my righteousness and will not release it' — ṣiḏqāṯî heḥĕzaqtî wĕlōʾ ʾarp̄ehā = my righteousness I have grasped and will not let go. The language of 'grasping/holding fast' (ḥāzaq) is the same used for faith-commitment in Isa 41:10 ('I will uphold you with my righteous right hand') and Heb 10:23 ('hold fast the confession of our hope'). 'My heart does not condemn me for a single day I have lived' (wĕlōʾ-yiḥrap̄ lĕḇāḇî miyāmāy) — ḥārap̄ = to reproach/upbraid; Job's inner tribunal (lēḇāḇ) pronounces him not guilty for the entire span of his life. This is the OT precedent for the NT concept of a conscience not condemning (1 John 3:21).
27.7: 'Let my enemy be treated as the wicked, and whoever rises against me as the unrighteous' — the imprecation shifts Job's discourse temporarily to the fate of his opponents. In the Psalms of lament, the imprecation against enemies is a standard element (Ps 35:4-8; 109:6-20; 137:7-9). The function of the imprecation is not personal vengeance but appeals to divine justice: the psalmist/Job is asking God to act consistently — if the wicked are to be punished, let those who wrongly accuse Job receive what is due the genuinely wicked. The imprecation is simultaneously a profession of innocence.
27.8: 'For what hope does the godless man have?' (mah-tiqwaṯ ḥānēp̄) — the hope-vocabulary (tiqwāh) that Job applied to himself in 6:8 and 14:19 is here denied to the godless (ḥānēp̄ = the impious/profane one, Job's term for the religious hypocrite in 8:13; 13:16; 17:8; 20:5). The juxtaposition is pointed: the godless man's hope is nothing when God removes the soul (nep̄ēš). This logic — earthly accumulation without God is groundless hope — is the theme of Jesus's rich fool parable (Luke 12:16-21) and the wisdom tradition's consistent warning against wealth as primary security.
27.9: 'Will God hear his cry when trouble comes upon him?' — the question inverts Job's own situation: Job cries to God and is not answered (30:20); the godless man also cries to God and will not be heard. The verse draws a distinction that is crucial for theodicy: both Job and the godless suffer and cry out, but their situations differ in what they have done with God during prosperity. The godless man has not cultivated relationship with God (v10: 'does he call upon God at all times?'), so his cry in adversity falls into the relational void he created.
27.10: 'Does he take delight in the Almighty?' (ʾim-ʿal-Šaddai yitʿannāg) — ʿānaḡ (to delight in/take pleasure in; used in Ps 37:4 'delight yourself in the LORD'; Isa 58:14 'then you will delight in the LORD'). The delight-in-God vocabulary is the positive content of the fear-of-God framework: wisdom is not merely cognitive acknowledgment but affective relationship, characterized by delight and regular address. 'Does he call upon God at all times?' — the ʿet-kol-ʿēṯ (at all times) of continuous prayer is the wisdom ideal (Ps 34:1 'I will bless the LORD at all times'; 1 Thess 5:17 'pray without ceasing').
27.11: 'I will instruct you about the hand of God' (ʾăyaḏ-ʾēl ʾăʾallep̄kem) — ʾālap̄ (to teach/instruct in pi'el; used in Prov 22:25 for teaching); the hand of God (yāḏ-ʾēl) as the agent of divine action, both providential care and punitive judgment. Job here claims the teacher's role that the friends have assumed throughout the dialogue: he will give the instruction (tôrāh) that the friends have been trying to give him, but his will be accurate. The verse marks a rhetorical inversion: the accused becomes the instructor of his accusers.
27.12: 'You yourselves have seen all this; why then have you become so thoroughly empty?' — hēn ʾattēm kullĕḵem ḥăzîṯem = look, all of you have seen; wĕlāmmāh-zeh hebel tihbālû = why then do you engage in complete emptiness? heḇel (vapor/emptiness/vanity, Ecclesiastes' central word) applied to the friends' elaborate theological arguments. The charge is not that they lack data but that their interpretation of data they have both seen is empty — their framework (retributive theology) is vapor that cannot hold the weight of Job's actual case.
27.13: Verse 13 begins a section (vv13-23) that closely parallels the three friends' descriptions of the wicked man's fate (Eliphaz in 15:17-35; Bildad in 18:5-21; Zophar in 20:4-29). This structural parallel has generated significant debate: some scholars argue the section is misattributed — that it was originally a third speech of Zophar (the missing speech from cycle 3) and was displaced in transmission. Others argue Job is deliberately using the friends' own theological language to describe those who are genuinely wicked (i.e., the friends themselves), turning their framework against them. The text as we have it attributes it to Job.
27.14: 'If his children multiply, it is only for the sword' — the doom of the wicked man's posterity is the reverse of the Abrahamic blessing-formula: rather than children being 'like the stars of the sky' (Gen 22:17), the wicked man's children multiply only to die violently. The four judgments on his family (sword, famine, plague, widows' grief) follow the standard OT judgment-sequence: Jer 14:12 and 15:2 use the same four agents (sword/famine/plague/captivity) as the Deuteronomic punishment pattern for covenant violation.
27.15: 'His survivors will be buried by plague' (śĕrîḏāyw bammāwet yiqqāḇērû) — śārid (survivor/remnant) buried in death, with no mourning widows weeping over them. The absence of mourning is among the most severe covenant-curses (Jer 16:4,6 'they will not be mourned or buried'; 22:18-19 for Jehoiakim: 'the burial of a donkey... dragged away and thrown outside Jerusalem'). Proper burial and mourning were essential to ancient Israelite social dignity; their denial was a form of posthumous disgrace.
27.16: 'Though he pile up silver like dust and lay up clothing as plentiful as clay' — the accumulation imagery (silver/ḵesep̄ and clothing/lĕḇûš) reflects the standard ANE wealth-assessment: silver by weight and textile stores by volume. In ancient economies where currency was pre-monetary, stored silver and textile inventory represented liquid and non-liquid wealth respectively. The 'like dust' (kĕʿāpār) simile for silver quantity hyperbolically inverts the normal value-hierarchy: what is precious becomes as common as dirt.
27.17: 'The righteous man will wear it, and the innocent will share out the silver' — the transfer of the wicked man's stored wealth to the righteous is a standard wisdom-theme and covenant-blessing formula: Prov 13:22 ('the sinner's wealth is stored up for the righteous'); 28:8 ('he who increases his wealth by charging interest accumulates it for one who will be kind to the poor'); Job 27:13-17 describes the complete reversal of fortune. The same motif governs Exod 3:22 (Israel plundering Egypt at the Exodus) as the transfer of stored wealth from the oppressor to the oppressed.
27.18: 'He builds his house as a moth builds its web, and like the temporary shelter a watchman puts up' — two fragility images: the moth's cocoon (ʿāš = moth; used in Isa 50:9; 51:8 for God destroying enemies 'like a garment the moth will eat') and the agricultural watchman's booth (śukkāh = the temporary harvest-season shelter; the same word as the Feast of Tabernacles/Sukkot booths, Lev 23:42-43). Both structures are intrinsically impermanent: the moth-web dissolves, the watchman's booth is abandoned after harvest. The wicked man's permanent-looking house has the internal structure of temporary materials.
27.19: 'The rich man lies down, but is not gathered to his fathers' — the normal Israelite death formula 'gathered to his people/fathers' (ne'esap̄ ʾel-ʿammāyw; Gen 25:8 for Abraham; 35:29 for Isaac; 49:33 for Jacob) is denied the wicked man. 'He opens his eyes, and is gone' — the sudden disappearance: in one sleep the accumulated wealth and social position have evaporated. The verse uses the minimal narrative contrast (lies down/opens eyes) to enact the sudden reversal: the space between lying down and waking is all the time the divine judgment requires to strip the wicked man.
27.20: 'Terrors sweep over him like a flood' (tĕśîgēhû kammayim ballāhôṯ) — the word ballāhôṯ (terrors) appears again — it is a key Joban term (9:34; 13:21; 15:24; 18:11,14; 24:17; 30:15). Job uses it of his own experience and now of the wicked man's fate; the same terrors that assail the innocent Job are presented as the just punishment of the wicked. 'A storm carries him off in the night' — the storm-judgment motif characteristic of ANE theophanic judgment (Amos 1:14; Nah 1:3; Hab 3:14).
27.21: 'The east wind sweeps him away and he is gone' — the qādîm (east wind) is the devastating desert sirocco that desiccates crops and humans alike; it is used in prophetic judgment texts as the agent of divine punishment (Isa 27:8; Jer 18:17; Ezek 17:10; 19:12; Hos 13:15). The east wind as divine weapon is an ANE theological commonplace: in Canaanite mythology the storm-winds served the divine warrior. In Egypt, the east wind brought the locusts of the plague (Exod 10:13). The wicked man's home from which he is flung (mimmĕqōmô = from his place) is the social position that the judgment dismantles.
27.22: 'God hurls his power at him without mercy' (wayyaššlēḵ ʿālāyw wĕlōʾ yaḥmōl) — šālak (to throw/hurl, the same word as throwing something aside or casting a stone); ḥāmal (to spare/have compassion). The combination of violent divine action and the explicit denial of mercy marks total judicial abandonment. In the Psalms, God's lack of mercy is the condition of covenant curse: Ps 109:12 ('let no one extend kindness to him'); Lam 2:2,17,21 for the destruction of Jerusalem where God 'did not spare.'
27.23: 'Men clap their hands at him and hiss him away from his home' — the two gestures of public contempt: ṣāpaq kap̄ (to clap hands; the gesture of contemptuous dismissal in Lam 2:15 'all who pass by clap their hands at you, O daughter Jerusalem'; Nah 3:19 'all who hear the news about you clap their hands') and šāraq (to hiss/whistle in contempt; used in Jer 19:8; 25:9,18 and Lam 2:15,16 for the same judgment-scenes). The social drama of public humiliation — shaming through gesture — is the final image of the wicked man's fall, completing the arc from private doom (v19-22) to public disgrace.

28.1: Chapter 28 is the theological pivot of the entire book — the Wisdom Hymn. Its genre is unique: a sustained hymn on the inaccessibility of wisdom, distinct from the surrounding dialogues. Many scholars consider it an independent poem inserted by the final editor as a theological commentary on the dialogue's failure to locate wisdom. The hymn proceeds in three movements: human mastery of mining (vv1-11), wisdom's inaccessibility to human searching (vv12-19), and God's exclusive knowledge of wisdom (vv20-28). The opening verse — 'surely silver has its mine' — begins with a survey of human technological achievement that was remarkable in the ancient world. Silver mining (kēsep̄ meqômô = silver has its place of origin) was a major industry in the ancient Near East.
28.2: Iron (barzel) taken from earth and copper (nĕḥûšāh) smelted from ore reflect the actual metallurgical reality of the Iron and Copper Ages. The Sinai peninsula's Timna Valley has copper mines that were worked from the 5th millennium BCE through the 12th century BCE (the Egyptian New Kingdom mining operations there are well documented). The Edomite copper operations south of the Dead Sea are referenced in Deut 8:9 ('a land whose rocks are iron and from whose hills you can dig copper'). The detail of the hymn suggests familiarity with the specific technologies of ancient mining.
28.3: 'A miner sets a limit to the darkness' (qēṣ śām laḥōšeḵ) — the miner penetrates ḥōšeḵ (darkness, the primal condition of Gen 1:2 before creation), driving back the darkness as a kind of inverse creation. The phrase ʿeḇen ʾōp̄el wĕṣalmāwet (stone of deep gloom and shadow of death) uses ṣalmāwet, Job's own darkness-word (3:5; 10:21-22; 12:22; 24:17; 28:3; 34:22), here applied to the geographical depth of mines rather than metaphorical suffering. The connection between mining-depth and death-darkness is both physical (mines as underworld) and symbolic.
28.4: The technical mining description: a shaft sunk 'far from where people live' (pāraṣ naḥal mēʿim gār = he breaks a watercourse away from habitation), 'forgotten by travelers' feet' (niškaḥû min-regel = forgotten from the foot). The miners 'dangle' (ḏallû) — a word meaning to be suspended/hang; the image of miners suspended in shafts on ropes is confirmed by ancient depictions of Egyptian and Sinai mining operations. The technique of shaft mining required workers to be lowered into deep vertical shafts, exactly as described.
28.5: 'The earth produces bread from its surface, but underneath it is turned up as though by fire' — the paradox of the cultivated earth: its surface produces bread (leḥem), the most basic food, while its underworld (underneath = taḥteyāh) is 'turned up as though by fire' (nehpāḵ kĕmô-ʾēš). The fire-below is the smelting process: the hymn sees the earth simultaneously producing crops above and being refined by mining fire below. The connection to the creation's dual nature — life-giving surface and dangerous depth — mirrors the human condition of surface blessing and depth-suffering that characterizes Job's own story.
28.6: 'Sapphires' — more likely lapis lazuli in the ancient context; the Hebrew term sappîr (from Akkadian sampu) referred to the brilliant blue stone that was lapis lazuli before true sapphires were known in the Near East. Lapis lazuli came from specific deposits in Badakhshan (northeast Afghanistan) and was among the most prized materials in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia; Egyptian furniture, the Mesopotamian 'standard of Ur,' and the Aegean Blue Bird frescoes all feature it. 'Flakes of gold' (ʿăp̄ārôṯ zāhāḇ) — gold dust in the earth alongside lapis: the mining hymn catalogs the precious materials only skilled human searching can find.
28.7: 'A path no bird of prey knows, unseen by the eye of the falcon' — the vision imagery: the falcon (ʾayyāh, likely the buzzard or kite) has the sharpest eyes of any creature (Prov 30:17); if even the falcon's eye has not found the mining path, the path is truly hidden from all aerial observation. The bird-of-prey's vision as the standard for exceptional sight also appears in 39:26-29 where God asks Job about the eagle's far-sight. The hymn establishes that the human mining path is invisible to the most acute natural vision — and by extension wisdom is even more hidden.
28.8: 'The lion's cubs have not walked it, nor the fierce lion passed over it' — the land predator (šaḥal = lion cub or young lion; ʿayiš = the lion in its fierce aspect) completes the survey of natural power: neither aerial power (falcon) nor terrestrial power (lion) has traversed the mining path. Together the two predator-types represent the peak of natural strength and ability — the mining path evades even them, just as wisdom evades all natural searches. The survey of creation's most capable creatures proves them all inadequate to locate wisdom.
28.9: 'He puts his hand to the flint' (šālaḥ yāḏô baḥallāmîš) — ḥallāmîš is flint/quartzite, the hardest readily available stone; working it requires percussive force (Deut 8:15; 32:13). 'Uproots mountains from their foundations' (hāpak mišōreš hārîm) — the hyperbole captures the scale of ancient mining: the hydraulic mining, tunneling, and blasting that created the massive Sinai copper mines or the silver mines of Laurion (Greece) involved engineering equivalent to uprooting mountains. Human technological ambition is celebrated here precisely to contrast it with the impossibility of acquiring wisdom by the same means.
28.10: 'He cuts channels through the rocks' (bĕṣûrôṯ yĕʾōrîm biqqēaʿ) — bāqaʿ (to split/cleave; used in Ps 78:13 for God splitting the Red Sea; in Isa 48:21 for water from the rock). The miner uses the same action as divine miracle-working: splitting rock to release water. 'His eye takes in every precious thing' (wĕḵol-yāqār rāʾǎṯāh ʿênô) — the comprehensive survey: after the splitting and searching, the miner sees (rāʾāh) every precious (yāqār, the same word as the precious topaz of Cush in v19 and the pricelessness of wisdom throughout the hymn) thing.
28.11: 'He holds back the streams from seeping through' (mibbĕkî nĕhārôṯ ḥibbēš) — the dam-building or plugging of water channels in mines is both a technical necessity (to prevent flooding) and a feat of engineering. The verse's final phrase: 'brings hidden things out to the light' (wĕtaʿalūmāh yōṣîʾ lāʾôr = the hidden thing he brings to the light) — taʿălumāh is from ʿālam (to be hidden, same root as Isa 45:15 'God of Israel, you are a God who hides himself'). The human miner can extract hidden physical things, but the hymn will go on to prove that wisdom — the most hidden thing — resists even this mastery.
28.12: 'But where shall wisdom be found?' (wĕhaḥāḵmāh mēʾayin timāṣēʾ) — the pivotal question after the survey of human technological achievement. The two questions (v12: where is wisdom? v13: no mortal knows its path) form the structural hinge of the hymn. The unanswerable quality of the question has generated extensive commentary in the wisdom tradition: Prov 30:1-4 ('where is wisdom? I do not know'), Sirach 24 (wisdom speaks in first person describing her origin), Baruch 3:9-4:4, and ultimately John 1:1-18 (the Logos who 'was with God' as the answer to where wisdom was before creation). The question Job 28:12 raises is one the OT leaves partly unanswered until the NT's identification of Christ as wisdom incarnate.
28.13: 'No mortal knows the path to it, and it is not found in the land of the living' (wĕlōʾ-nimṣāʾ bĕʾereṣ haḥayyîm) — ʾereṣ haḥayyîm (land of the living) is the standard OT phrase for earthly existence (Ps 27:13; 52:5; 116:9; Isa 38:11; 53:8). If wisdom is not in the land of the living, no amount of human searching there can find it. The syllogism is complete: humanity's most sophisticated technology can extract the earth's deepest physical materials (vv1-11) but cannot find wisdom. Physical achievement and wisdom-acquisition are categorically different operations.
28.14: 'The deep says: It is not in me' — the personification of the Deep (tĕhôm, the primordial abyss of Gen 1:2) denying possession of wisdom; the sea (yām) makes the same denial. In ANE mythology, the sea-depths were the dwelling of Yam (the sea deity) and the realm of hidden cosmic secrets; the hymn's claim that even the primordial watery chaos denies knowledge of wisdom's location is a significant theological assertion. The pre-creation chaos has no wisdom — wisdom pre-dates the ordering of creation and exists at a different ontological level than any cosmic region.
28.15: 'It cannot be bought with gold, and its price cannot be paid in silver' — the commercial language (ṭibbān-ʾeḵsep̄ = it cannot be acquired with silver; wĕlōʾ-yiššāqal kāsāp̄ meḥîrāh = its price cannot be weighed in silver) uses the market-vocabulary of weighing precious metals as currency. In the pre-coinage ancient economy, commerce was conducted by weighing silver and gold ingots. The wisdom-market operates on a different currency entirely: verse 28 will identify it as the fear of the LORD. This is the same logic as Isa 55:1-2 ('come, buy wine and milk without money and without cost... why spend money on what is not bread?').
28.16: Ophir (v16) — the most famous gold-source in the ancient world: 1 Kgs 9:28 (Solomon sends ships to Ophir for gold); 10:11 (gold, almug wood, and precious stones from Ophir); 22:48 (Jehoshaphat's failed Ophir expedition); Isa 13:12 ('more precious than the gold of Ophir'); Ps 45:9; Job 22:24. Ophir's location remains debated: proposals include southern Arabia (Oman/Yemen), the Horn of Africa (Somalia), East Africa (Mozambique), and India. The consistent association with long sea-voyages suggests a distant location accessed via the Red Sea. The point is that even the most legendary gold source cannot purchase wisdom.
28.17: 'Gold and crystal cannot match its worth' (lōʾ-yaʿarkennāh zāhāḇ ûzĕḵôḵîṯ) — zĕḵôḵîṯ (crystal/glass; from zāḵāh = to be clear/pure); glass was extremely rare and precious in the ancient world — most transparent material was actually rock crystal (quartz). The Egyptians had glass from ca. 1500 BCE but production was limited and colored glass was more common. Rock crystal and early glass objects were luxury items ranking with precious stones. 'Fine gold' (kĕlî p̄āz = vessels of refined gold) — p̄āz is a special poetic word for the finest gold (Ps 19:10; 21:3; Song 5:11; Prov 8:19 — notably, Prov 8:19 has Wisdom herself saying 'my fruit is better than fine gold/p̄āz').
28.18: 'Coral and crystal are not even worth mentioning' (rāmôṯ wĕgāḇîš lōʾ-yizzāḵēr) — rāmôṯ (coral, red coral being a luxury trade item; Ezek 27:16 lists coral in Tyre's trade inventory with Aram); gāḇîš (crystal/alabaster, possibly). 'The value of wisdom is above rubies' (mešeḵ ḥāḵmāh mippənînîm) — pĕnînîm (rubies or pearls; Prov 3:15 'she is more precious than rubies'; 8:11; 31:10; Lam 4:7). The comprehensive catalog of the ancient world's most precious materials — silver, gold, Ophir-gold, lapis lazuli, fine gold, coral, crystal, rubies — all fail the comparison. Wisdom exceeds all enumerable value.
28.19: The topaz of Cush (piṭdaṯ-kûš) — Cush is the biblical designation for the Nubian/Ethiopian region south of Egypt. The Nubian empire (25th Dynasty of Egypt, ca. 747-656 BCE) controlled territories rich in gold and gemstones; the topaz (piṭdāh, possibly green peridot rather than modern yellow topaz) was a characteristic gem of the African trade. Its identification as coming specifically from Cush attests to genuine trade knowledge. The verse closes the precious-materials list with 'it cannot be priced against pure gold' (wĕḵettem tāhôr lōʾ yaʿarkennāh) — kéttem (poetic term for finest gold; cf. Ps 45:9).
28.20: 'Where then does wisdom come from?' (wĕhaḥāḵmāh mēʾayin tāḇôʾ) — the question of v12 is reprised with a different second verb: timāṣēʾ (will it be found?) in v12 vs. tāḇôʾ (will it come?) in v20. The variation is significant: wisdom in v20 is not just waiting to be found but actively arriving — it is not a static deposit but a dynamic arrival. This prepares for v28's answer: God does not just know where wisdom is stored; he imparts it to those who fear him. The repetition creates the hymn's structural symmetry (A: vv1-11; B: vv12-19; A': vv20-28), with v20 as the reprise.
28.21: 'It is hidden from the eyes of every living creature and kept from the sight of the birds of the sky' — the negative reversal of the hymn's bird-imagery: in v7 the falcon's eye could not see the miners' path; here even that failure is exceeded — wisdom is hidden not just from falcons but from 'every living creature' (kol-ḥāy = every living being) and 'the birds of the sky' (ʿôp̄ haššāmayim). The escalation from animal failure to complete creaturely failure prepares the divine claim of v23-27: only God sees and knows.
28.22: 'Abaddon and Death say: We have heard only a rumor of it' — the personified underworld realms of ʾăḇaddôn and māwet (Death) have heard only a šēmaʿ (report/rumor/hearing) about wisdom. Abaddon (from ʾāḇad = to perish) is the realm of destruction below Sheol (26:6; 31:12; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11; Rev 9:11 where the angel of the bottomless pit is named Abaddon). Even the underworld — which receives all the dead and might be expected to accumulate all knowledge — has only heard about wisdom secondhand. The rumor-knowledge of the underworld contrasts with God's direct, complete knowledge in v23.
28.23: 'God understands the way to it and knows its location' — ʾĕlōhîm hēḇîn darkāh wĕhûʾ yāḏaʿ ʾeṯ-mĕqômāh. After the comprehensive negative survey — all creation, the depths, the sea, the sky, all creatures, even the underworld, none know wisdom — the positive claim arrives: God alone has this knowledge. The verse uses the two wisdom-verbs: bîn (to understand/discern) and yāḏaʿ (to know); God has both the rational comprehension and the relational knowledge of wisdom's location. This exclusive divine knowledge will be opened to humanity only in v28 through the specific channel of the fear of the LORD.
28.24: 'For he looks to the ends of the earth and sees everything under all of heaven' — kî-hûʾ liṣpôṯ hāʾāreṣ taḥaṯ kol-haššāmayim yirʾeh. The divine panoramic vision (liṣpôṯ = to look out/survey from a height; yirʾeh = sees) encompasses the full spatial extent of creation: ends of earth + all under heaven. The verse grounds God's wisdom-knowledge in his comprehensive visual access — he sees what no creature sees because no creature stands at the vantage point from which all creation is visible simultaneously. The divine all-seeing is the epistemological foundation of divine wisdom.
28.25: 'When he assigned a weight to the wind and measured the waters' — the creation-measuring activity: lĕʿăśôṯ lārûaḥ mišqāl = making a weight for the wind; ûmayim tikkēn bĕmiddāh = measuring the waters with measure. The concept of winds and waters having assigned weights and measures reflects the ancient understanding of creation as precisely calibrated (Isa 40:12 'who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand?'; Prov 8:29 where Wisdom watches God set the sea's limits). These are the same measurements the divine speech from the whirlwind (ch38-41) will deploy against Job in its series of unanswerable questions.
28.26: 'When he established a decree for the rain and a path for the lightning and thunder' (ḇĕʿăśōṯô lammāṭār ḥōq ûḏāreḵ lăḥăzîz qōlôṯ) — ḥōq for the rain is a divine statute/ordinance (the same word for law/decree throughout Pentateuchal legislation); the rain operates according to divine legal-administrative regulation, not random occurrence. 'Path for the lightning and thunder' — the lightning bolt has a designated track, like a road. The creation-measuring of vv25-26 is the OT background for the NT claim that all things were 'created through him and for him' and 'hold together in him' (Col 1:16-17).
28.27: 'Then he saw it and made it known; he established it and fully searched it out' — ʾāz rāʾāh wayyĕsappĕrāh hĕḵînāh wĕgam-ḥăqārāh. Four verbs in sequence: saw (rāʾāh), made it known (sāpar = declared/narrated), established (kûn = set in order), searched out (ḥāqar = investigated/audited). The final verb ḥāqar is used for wisdom-research throughout the OT (Prov 25:2; Job 28:27; 29:16; 1 Chr 28:9); by 'fully searching out' wisdom in the act of creation, God performed the definitive wisdom-audit. When God built wisdom into creation's structure, he performed the comprehensive investigation no creature can replicate.
28.28: 'To mankind he said: The fear of the Lord is wisdom, and to turn from evil is understanding' — the hymn's climactic conclusion. Yirʾaṯ ʾăḏōnāy hîʾ ḥāḵmāh — the formula 'fear of the LORD = wisdom' is the foundational axiom of the entire OT Wisdom tradition: Prov 1:7 ('the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom'); 9:10; Ps 111:10; Eccl 12:13. The personalized address to 'mankind' (lāʾāḏām) connects to the Adam-language of Gen 2-3: the wisdom that was sought in the fruit of the tree (Gen 3:6 'desirable for gaining wisdom') and found to be inaccessible by human initiative is here given as a gift through the relational posture of fear and moral turning. Job 28:28 is the OT's most concentrated statement of wisdom-as-gift rather than wisdom-as-achievement.

29.1: 'Job continued his discourse' — wayyōsep̄ ʾîyyôḇ śēʾt mĕšālô, the same formula as 27:1. Chapters 29-31 form Job's final speech — a three-part reflection: ch29 (the past: what I was), ch30 (the present: what I am now), ch31 (the oath of clearance: what I have not done). This is the most formally structured section of Job's dialogues, with a deliberate tripartite architecture. The nostalgic mood of ch29 is the literary device of the 'golden age' lament, paralleling similar retrospective laments in Sumerian literature ('Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur') and the Psalms (Ps 42:4; 77:5-10: 'I remember the days of long ago').
29.2: 'As in the months gone by, in the days when God watched over me' — kĕyarḥê qeḏem (as in months of ancient/before), the months of the past. The specific wish is not just for restored circumstances but for restored relationship: 'when God watched over me' (bîmê ʾĕlôah šĕmārānî). šāmar (to watch/guard/keep) is the covenantal protection-verb: God 'watches over' (šāmar) Israel in Num 6:24-26 (the Aaronic blessing); Ps 121:3-8 ('he who watches over you will not slumber'); the same verb of covenant-keeping applies in Prov 2:8 and throughout the Psalms. God's 'watching' of Job has become the oppressive surveillance of 7:17-20; here Job nostalgically remembers when it was protective.
29.3: 'His lamp shone on my head and by his light I walked through darkness' — nērô (his lamp); ʾôrô (his light); ḥōšeḵ (darkness). The divine lamp as metaphor for favor, guidance, and life appears throughout: Ps 18:28 ('you, LORD, keep my lamp burning; my God turns my darkness into light'); Prov 6:23 ('a lamp and a light is the law'); 2 Sam 22:29; the Tabernacle lamp (Exod 25:37) as the divine light-presence in the midst of the camp. The image of walking through darkness by God's light anticipates the NT's 'your word is a lamp for my feet' (Ps 119:105) and 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12).
29.4: 'As I was in the prime of life' (kaʾăšer hāyîṯî biymê ḥorpî) — ḥōrep̄ is the harvest season/autumn, used here as a metaphor for the best years of adulthood (as 'the autumn of life' in some cultures represents mature prime). 'The friendship of God rested upon my tent' — sôḏ ʾĕlôah ʿălê ʾohŏlî. sôḏ means intimate counsel/the secret council (the same word as the divine council in Jer 23:18,22 'stood in the council/sôḏ of YHWH'; Ps 25:14 'the LORD confides in those who fear him'; Amos 3:7 'he reveals his sôḏ to his servants the prophets'). Job recalls a time when God's intimate counsel rested on his household — the deepest possible description of divine favor.
29.5: 'When the Almighty was still with me' — bĕʿôḏ Šaddai ʿimmāḏî: the spatial-relational 'with me' (ʿimmāḏî) is the covenant-presence formula: God 'with' his people (Gen 28:15 to Jacob; Exod 3:12 to Moses; Josh 1:5 to Joshua; Isa 43:2 'when you pass through the waters I will be with you'). The formula 'with me' expresses the covenantal companionship that Job now experiences as broken. 'My children gathered around me' — the image of children surrounding the patriarch at table (Ps 128:3 'your children around your table like olive shoots') as the visible sign of covenant blessing.
29.6: 'I bathed my steps in cream and the rock poured out streams of oil' — reḥōṣ hălîḵāy beḥemāʾ wĕṣûr yāṣûq ʿimmî p̄alğê-šāmen. Two hyperboles of surplus: cream (ḥemʾāh = curds/butter, the richest dairy product) for the feet's path, and oil (šāmen = olive oil) streaming from the rock. These are the paradigmatic foods of covenant blessing in Deut 32:13-14 ('honey from the rock... curds and milk from herd and flock; with the finest kernels of wheat'). Olive oil flowing from rock is the paradox of abundance that transcends natural limitation — the wilderness rock that flowed with water (Exod 17:6; Num 20:8-11) here flows with oil.
29.7: 'When I went out to the city gate and took my seat in the public square' — the city gate (šaʿar) as the administrative, judicial, and commercial center of the ancient city (Gen 19:1; Deut 21:19; Ruth 4:1-11; Prov 31:23). Taking a seat (kûn môšāḇ = establish a seat/position) in the public square (rĕḥōḇ) was the formal act of taking judicial or public-advisory position. The scene Job describes — arriving at the gate to a reception of honor from young men, elders, princes, and nobles — follows the precise social hierarchy of an ancient Israelite city, and will be exactly inverted in 30:1-10.
29.8: The honor-reception sequence: young men step aside (nĕʿārîm rāʾûnî wĕniṯḥabbāʾû = young men saw me and hid/stepped aside), aged men rose and stood (wĕyĕšîšîm qāmû ʿāmādû). In ancient honor culture, the young stepping aside for an elder, and the aged themselves rising from their seats for a distinguished person, were the highest social recognitions. The elders who rise to stand for Job reverse the normal protocol — usually it is youth that rises for age, not the reverse. This hyperbolically describes Job's honor as exceeding even that of the aged sages.
29.9: 'The princes stopped speaking and covered their mouths with their hands' — śārîm ʿāṣĕrû bĕmillîm = princes restrained their words; kap̄ śāmû lĕp̄îhem = they set their hands to their mouths. The gesture of hand-over-mouth is the ANE posture of awed or respectful silence; it appears in Judg 18:19 (as a command to silence) and is the gesture Elihu says he avoided (29:9b) and the gesture Job himself makes in 40:4 ('I put my hand over my mouth') when God speaks from the whirlwind. The princes are silenced not by terror but by reverence for Job's wisdom and authority.
29.10: 'The voice of the nobles fell silent; their tongues stuck to the roof of their mouths' — qôl nĕgîḏîm neʾĕsāmāh = the voice of the nobles was checked; ûlĕšônām leḥiḵām ḏāḇēqāh = their tongues adhered to their palates. The tongue-adhering-to-palate is an idiomatic expression for complete silence combined with inability to speak (Ps 22:15 of the sufferer; Lam 4:4 of thirsty children). Job uses it to describe the quality of silence his presence commanded: the nobles' normal eloquence was physically arrested. The contrast with ch30:9 ('and now I am their song and byword') is the sharpest possible social reversal.
29.11: 'For the ear that heard me called me blessed, and the eye that saw me testified for me' — kî ʾōzen šāmĕʿāh wattĕʾaššĕrenî = the ear that heard me called me blessed; wĕʿayin rāʾǎṯāh wattĕʿîḏēnî = the eye that saw me testified on my behalf. The beatitude-language (ʾišśĕrennî = declared me ʾašrê-blessed, the happiness-formula of Ps 1:1 and Matt 5:3-12) and the legal-testimony language (ʿûḏ = to witness/testify) combine: Job's behavior produced both social blessing-acclamation and legal vindication. He was not merely admired but formally attested by those who saw and heard him.
29.12: 'For I rescued the poor when they cried for help, and the fatherless who had no one to help them' — kî ʾămalleṭ ʿānî mĕšawwēaʿ = I rescued the afflicted man who cried for help; wĕyātôm wĕlōʾ-ʿōzēr lô = and the fatherless who had no helper. The specific trilogy of vulnerable persons — poor (ʿānî), fatherless (yātôm), widow (ʾalmānāh, v13) — is the canonical test-case for social righteousness throughout the OT. Exod 22:21-24 makes their protection a direct divine concern; Isa 1:17 makes it the criterion for authentic religion; and James 1:27 makes it the definition of 'pure and undefiled religion before God.' Job is claiming he passed this test comprehensively.
29.13: 'The blessing of the one about to perish came to me, and I made the widow's heart sing for joy' — birkat ʾōḇēḏ ʿālay tāḇōʾ = the blessing of the perishing came to me; wĕlēḇ ʾalmānāh ʾărannen = I caused the widow's heart to sing for joy. Two complementary blessings: the active (given by the perishing/rescued person) and the passive (the joy that overflows in the widow's heart). The 'song for joy' (rānan) of the widow's heart is the spontaneous worship-response to justice: the same verb used in Ps 5:11 ('let them sing for joy') and Isa 12:6 ('sing aloud'). Genuine justice creates genuine worship.
29.14: 'I put on righteousness and it clothed me; my justice was like a robe and a turban' — ṣeḏeq lāḇaštî wayyilbāšēnî = I put on righteousness and it clothed me; kĕmĕʿîl ûṣānîp̄ mišpāṭî = my justice was like a robe and a turban. The vestment metaphor for righteousness is significant: clothing connotes identity, social role, and consecration. The robe (mĕʿîl = the priestly or royal outer garment; 1 Sam 24:4; Ezra 9:3; the high priest's robe in Exod 28:4) and turban (ṣānîp̄ = the priestly or royal head-turban; Exod 29:9; Zech 3:5; Isa 62:3) make Job's righteousness priestly-royal in its quality. The NT develops this: Isa 61:10 uses the same robe/garland imagery for the redeemed; Rev 19:8 ('fine linen stands for the righteous acts of God's holy people').
29.15: 'I was eyes for the blind and feet for the lame' — ʿênayim hāyîṯî laʿiwwēr wĕraglayim lappissēaḥ ʾānî = I was eyes for the blind man, and feet for the lame man. This verse stands at the convergence of OT social ethics and Messianic prophecy. Isaiah 35:5-6 describes the age of salvation: 'then the eyes of the blind will be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped; then the lame will leap like a deer.' When John the Baptist sends disciples to ask Jesus 'are you the one who is to come?', Jesus answers by citing precisely these signs: 'the blind receive sight, the lame walk, the deaf hear, the dead are raised' (Matt 11:4-5; Luke 7:22). Job's righteous ministry embodies the pattern that the Messiah will fulfill eschatologically.
29.16: 'I was a father to the needy, and I investigated the case of the one I did not know' — ʾāḇ ʾānōḵî lāʾeḇyônîm = I was a father to the destitute; wĕrîḇ lōʾ-yāḏaʿtî ʾeḥqĕrēhû = and the case of one I did not know I investigated. The 'father of the poor' is a royal and priestly title in the ANE: Egyptian pharaohs and Mesopotamian kings described themselves as 'fathers of the poor' in royal ideology. Job's self-description as father of the destitute places him in this tradition of responsible power. The legal investigation (ḥāqar = to research/investigate, same root as the wisdom-research of 28:27) of unknown cases shows proactive justice: Job did not wait for cases to come to him.
29.17: 'I broke the fangs of the wicked and forced the prey from his teeth' — ʾăšabberāh mĕṭallĕʿôṯ ʿawwal = I broke the fangs of the unjust man; ûmin-šinnāyw ʾašlîḵ tāref̄ = and I threw the prey from his teeth. The predator-imagery for the oppressor (wicked man as lion holding prey in its teeth) and the justice-intervention as forced-restitution is vivid. The lion-fang metaphor appears in Ps 3:7 ('strike all my enemies on the jaw; break the teeth of the wicked'); the active justice of forcibly removing what the powerful have taken from the vulnerable is the anti-prey logic of Amos 1-2 (the prophetic indictments of nations for oppression).
29.18: 'Then I said: I shall die in my own nest and multiply my days like the sand' — ʾāmartî ʿim-qinnî ʾegwaʿ = I said: in my nest I shall expire; wĕkĕḥôl ʾarbeh yāmîm = and as the sand I shall multiply my days. The nest (qēn = nest, used for birds' nests and human homes as places of security and children; Prov 27:8; Num 24:21; Isa 10:14) as image of secure old-age death. The word ḥôl (usually 'sand') has generated significant ancient interpretation: the Septuagint and some Aramaic Targums read it as 'phoenix' (the mythological bird that dies and rises from its ashes), giving the verse a resurrection connotation. The Phoenix legend was known in Egypt and Greece; some ancient readers understood Job here to be expressing hope in a post-mortem renewal.
29.19: 'My roots spread out to the waters, and the dew rested all night on my branches' — šōrĕšî p̄āṯûaḥ ʾĕlê-māyim = my root opens/spreads to the waters; wĕṭal yālîn biqṣîrî = dew lodges in my branch. The tree-root-to-water image is the standard biblical metaphor for the righteous person firmly sustained by God: Ps 1:3 ('like a tree planted by streams of water which yields its fruit in season'); Jer 17:8 ('he will be like a tree planted by the water that sends out its roots by the stream'). Job's self-description as a water-connected, dew-bedewed tree captures the ideal of continuous divine sustenance that characterized his former life.
29.20: 'My glory remained fresh within me, and my bow was always renewed in my hand' — kĕḇôḏî ḥāḏāš ʿimmāḏî = my glory fresh with me; wĕqaštî bĕyāḏî taḥălîp̄ = my bow in my hand renewed. The bow (qešeṯ) as symbol of military power and social authority: Gen 49:24 uses it for Joseph ('his bow remained steady'); the high priest's installation involved weapons as well as robes. 'Always renewed' (taḥălîp̄ = continually changes/renews; from ḥālap̄ = to pass through/renew) describes continuous rejuvenation rather than static possession — Job's power was not a fixed asset but constantly refreshed. The contrast with ch30's complete enfeeblement will be total.
29.21: 'Men listened to me; they waited and held their silence for my counsel' — lî-šāmĕʿû wĕyiḥēllû = for me they listened and waited; wĕyiddĕmû lĕmôʿāṯî = and fell silent for my counsel. The waiting (yāḥal = to hope/wait) and silence (dāmam = to be still/silent) before Job's speech captures the reverential anticipation of the sage's audience.
